Yet for all its brilliance, the hermeneutic remains retrospective. The critic can reconstruct the logic of the fall of man in the Bible, but the critic did not cause the plummet. The critic can explain the symbol, but is not enduring the lived reality from which it exists, such that it is conveyed in the form firstly encountered in the text. The genius of criticism is to comprehend a world already made, not in making the world anew, nor in creation in general. Steiner’s project, therefore, is not to be a better critic, but to become a potential author, and train you to become one as well. The change lies in the move from perfecting interpretation to developing the faculties to participate in the “writing” (as a matter of metaphor, of course). Enough has been done to analyze the divine logic, but not enough has been done to collaborate with the divine author.
In laying out the nature of truth, being, and the general arc of my narrative, there are bound to be accusations levied at me from a Hegelian flavoring. The first is a common one. It asserts that Steiner’s quest for real-time, participatory knowledge fails to grasp that retrospective comprehension is not a philosophical limitation, but the very form in which Truth actualizes itself, and that what he calls a “higher” activity is already the immanent, self-unfolding work of Concept. This is correct, however what is not grasped is that anthroposophy, as explained above, addresses a different activity altogether. Steiner fully acknowledges that retrospection is the mode of philosophical comprehension. His critique is that this mode is passive in relation to the creative act. The Concept’s “self-unfolding work” in this case is logically reconstructed after the fact by the philosopher. There is a necessary addition found in the capacity to co-participate in the unfolding as it occurs, such that Concept is not actually constitutive of that self-unfolding work. In other words, retrospective comprehension is the form of Truth-as-Known, but anthroposophy aims at Truth-as-Lived-Creation. The former is certainly necessary, but insufficient for the consciousness that seeks not just to understand Spirit, but to work with and within it.
Sidenote: It is almost inevitable that the philosophically minded (meant as an insult) will hear the words “reflection” or “reflective act” and construct a straw man to attack as if I am operating with the premature logic of Essence that Hegel dispenses with by the time the system develops to the Concept. The individual capable of laying out the whole path of Hegel’s philosophy; one capable of understanding the distinction between Essence and Concept, is surely someone with enough expertise to not make such a blunder in thought. Certainly I am no expert, but absolutely not a novice. It reminds me of that one tweet that goes, “Little Caesars tastes so good when you don’t have a b—- in your ear telling you it’s nasty”, only it’s to the tune of “Talking with people feels so good when you don’t have people intentionally misinterpreting you”.
Another accusation is in the same form as the previous, that the thinker is somehow misguided and not accounting for how the Concept already subsumes what one is saying. “Is Steiner not simply mistaking the acquisition of new perceptual content (or spiritual data) for the development of an entirely new faculty, when from a Hegelian standpoint any new immediacy is merely fresh material for the one sovereign faculty of thinking and Reason to mediate and comprehend”? This may sound devastating, and it is. For the Hegelian. For the Steinerian, the claim that “any new material is fresh material for thinking” proves his or her point! It confines all experience within the form of reflective thought. Steiner insists that spiritual perception is not just new content, but a qualitatively different relationship to reality, where the perceiver does not stand over and against an object, but consciously lives within the being of that object. The faculty is new because it overcomes the subject-object distinction inherent in sense-perception and logical reflection (and if the position is held that Hegel also overcomes the subject-object divide in philosophy, then at least it overcomes it in a way previously not standardized before). It is a participatory knowing, not an observational thinking. To call this “new material” is to reduce an active to a passive.
One final accusation is a rather dogmatic retort. It comes in the form of the assertion that in the encounter of spiritual reality, take Christ for example, the Hegelian will assert that the reality experienced of a Christ encounter, is entirely negotiable with the Concept, or Hegelian Idea. This groundless response is the last refuge for someone who truly does not understand what is at stake. Not only was this position dealt with in the “The Problem of Qualitative Immediacy”, it is not honest. The full, honest, position is that super-sensible reality, such as Christ encounters, are either mistaken contingencies or merely the raw material for the Concept’s self-unfolding. Imagine telling someone who has had a Christ encounter that they did not meet a person, but a logical predicate in the dialectic of the Absolute. They would rightly respond: “I didn’t experience a ‘Concept’, I experienced Christ!”. The Hegelian, committed to translating this encounter upward into the self-mediation of Reason, is thereby committed to alienating the very person whose experience they claim to comprehend. Keep in mind, this is not a vindication of anti-intellectualism, or an appeal to colloquial understandings, but a clear signal that the disconnect between the systematic “knower” and the so-called simple believer reveals either a fundamental disagreement about the object itself or a total failure of translation. Both of which, exhibit an ineptitude that should be addressed.
Hegel is a Kantian?!? Regarding the “outrageous” claim that Hegel is a Kantian, it is not my intention to re-inscribe the problems of Kant’s era in calling Hegel a Kantian. He is not Kantian in his conclusions, but in his philosophical gesture. Hegel establishes the absolute sovereignty and constitutive power of thinkability. He completes Kant’s project by collapsing the noumenal/phenomenal divide, but only by making the thinkable (Concept) synonymous with the real. For Kant, the categories of understanding structure our knowledge of phenomena, leaving the thing-in-itself beyond reach. For Hegel, the categories of thought generate the very structure of being itself, and so there is no beyond for thought. However, this triumph comes at a cost identified by the critiques above. The system is a perfectly closed system of thinkable reality, a logical phenomenology. In this, he remains within Kant’s formal constraint; he equates reality with what can be grasped by the (now cosmic, as Steiner calls it) subject’s apparatus, or the global function of thought. This cannot account for the living, creative territory of spiritual beings that precede and exceed, but are still in part constituted by, logical representation. Hegel, in trying to contend with Kantianism, has produced the Absolute Idea, which is just as much a phenomenon contingent on noumena, as Kant’s thing-in-itself.
To end on a high note, a salient point to emphasize is that Steiner does not negate Hegel, but seeks to fulfill him. Their projects share a common origin in the rupture diagnosed by Kant, and a common goal in the realization of human freedom. Both thinkers operate within surprisingly triadic movements: immediacy, mediation, and self mediation. This is the recursive rhythm of life and spirit. For Hegel, this logic culminates in the self-determination of the Absolute Idea. For Steiner, this same logic demands the actual practice of freedom, the development of faculties that allows man to participate consciously in the spiritual being that weaves the world. This is a shared Kantian inheritance. The difference is that Anthroposophy is a call to make the Hegelian system’s conclusion into a starting point. In turning the perfect map of Spirit’s past into a toolkit for navigating the living, creative future, we don’t merely comprehend freedom, but actively achieve it.
Dao De Jing. Chapter 14. Composite translation based on Bruce R. Linnell’s Dao De Jing: A Minimalist Translation, 2015, and Stephen Addiss and Stanley Lombardo’s Lao-tzu: Tao Te Ching, Hackett Publishing, 1993, p. 14. ↩︎
Frye, Northrop. Anatomy of Criticism: Four Essays. Princeton UP, 1957. ↩︎
Hegel, Georg Wilhelm Friedrich. Encyclopedia of the Philosophical Sciences in Basic Outline. Part I: Science of Logic. Specifically, §95 on the https://www.hegel.net website. ↩︎
The Bible. King James Version ↩︎
https://empyreantrail.wordpress.com ↩︎
Goethe, Johann Wolfgang von. “Chorus Mysticus.” Faust, Part Two. Translated by Philip Wayne. Penguin Classics, 1959. LiederNet Archive, www.lieder.net/lieder/get_text.html?TextId=49341. Accessed 10 Dec. 2025. ↩︎
Steiner, Rudolf. Goethes Weltanschauung. Rudolf Steiner Verlag, 1979. Gesamtausgabe (GA), vol. 6. —. Die Philosophie der Freiheit. Rudolf Steiner Verlag, 1978. Gesamtausgabe (GA), vol. 4. —. Von Jesus zu Christus. Rudolf Steiner Verlag, 1989. Gesamtausgabe (GA), vol. 108. ↩︎
Goethe, Johann Wolfgang von. Die Metamorphose der Pflanzen / The Metamorphosis of Plants. Edited by Gordon L. Miller, Getrud W. Miller, MIT Press, 2009. ↩︎
title: From Hegel to Steiner

content:
Non-Euclidean Revolution In a recent unfruitful conversation with Hegelians, I was made to defend the honor of non-Euclidean geometry, despite not being all that invested in the enterprise. The conditions of the argument sought to clarify the character of Non-Euclidean geometry, such that its very possibility was to be established as a whole. The proving grounds for such a position has to come from the conceptual determinations of space. Reason being that geometry is what is known as a science of the Understanding, it is a science with its chief concern being formal validity and symbolic coherence, namely, an avoidance of formal contradiction. No more in geometry does the Euclidean serve as proof that space is Euclidean than Non-Euclidean does. All it does is prove that geometry does not come from observing physical space; it shows that geometry originates in pure thinking rather than empirical observation. Therefore, geometry as a domain exists at a level abstracted beyond the determinations of space. Mathematical systems as a whole are products of the structuring activity of thought, the mind can generate multiple internally consistent “spatial” logics, and so it can be said that mathematics creates conceptual structures by which we engage with (as do all worldviews; see: Mathematism). “Science” then applies these structures to nature, and thus we have the judgment that mathematics models nature, but does not necessarily disclose its essence. It can be said that mathematics is thought grasping pure form independent of sensory experience. Truly, math alone does not penetrate deeper into the spiritual reality of the world, although we will return to this later.
It is evident then, that the possibility of a Non-Euclidean space lies within the determinations of space, which geometry takes as its supposition. Here is where the argument has taken place. Ultimately, the claim I was presented with was that inherent in the immanent development of space was Euclid’s five, yes you heard that right, five postulates, in the form of the claim that dimensions imply orthogonality. Now, I tend to be open to how meaning is disclosed to me. However, the only way in which orthogonality can be bound with dimensionality such that it would prove Euclid’s 5th postulate, is if the argument collapses the distinction between opposed as distinct, or dependent, into orthogonal, meaning at right angles. Orthogonality requires a metric, dimensions are tangent vectors to one another.
The argument leaps from the claim that dimensions are distinct, working at the manifold level, to “therefore perpendiculars exist globally”, working at the metric level. This very assumption is what needs proving, and the reason Euclid could not draw out the fifth postulate from the first four. For Hegel with respect to space, one begins with an immediate and indifferent self-externality. The only interruption of this self-externality can be ideal negations of it. For the purposes of brevity, we may consider point, although it is possible to begin with plane. Points of space are ideal insofar as their negation of extension cannot rest upon any thing in space, because this would be taking the space of that thing for granted. Each point is indifferent and indistinguishable to another, thereby uniting each indifferently with the next. The negative content of both moments is expelled, generating line. Because line is just as indifferent as space, space follows the same pattern from point and line and generates plane. In the words of Richard Dien Winfield, “space’s internal differentiation into point and line entails planes, whose own analogous similitude fills the continuity of space with the unbounded extension of spacial dimensions, which are equally indistinguishable from one another. It makes little difference whether height, length, and breadth be construed in Euclidean space or in a non-Euclidean space, whose use of curvilinear axes already presupposes the two and three-dimensionality defined by straight lines. In each case, what a dimension is identified as is a matter of indifference, since every one can substitute for the others without altering its internal or external relations.” Nothing in the manifold being of space, be it the indifferent distinctions and coexistence of dimensionality, or the for itself indifference of time (it’s ideal logical commencement), implies that a construction of the dimensions involves more than linear independence, not orthogonality. Any more would be an overreach. In my position, the conditions under which one would concede the opposing side would be a proof of the logical necessity of the fifth postulate within the determinations of space. However, as it stands, the conflation between a conceptual opposition, orthogonality, and the parallel postulate is never justified, only asserted. Even Hegel would not make such a bold claim, given that he was highly critical of everything. It also has to be said that in the domain of geometry, the proof has been all but been successful, particularly with Gödel’s incompleteness theorem problematizing, in part, first order axioms and formalist systems, and the explorations of Riemann, Bolyai, and Lobachevsky establish geometries which do not have the fifth postulate holding. Additionally, a position which holds that space as a whole is Euclidean, is a presumptuous position stating that all the work done which is non-Euclidean is either some fluke, intentional misdirection, or worthless. In any case, there is a very prideful and overreaching Moloch behind this claim.
Consequently, the distinction should be made, and has been made by Lawrence S Stepelevich, between what is known as “pure” geometry, and “physical” geometry. Hegel recognized this very distinction, comprehending “what the logic of the understanding has sundered into two exclusive geometries, one developed from an abstract logical content, the other deriving its content from the concrete contingencies of sensuous intuition”. Stepelevich argues that Hegel’s theory of geometry provides speculative support for both Euclidean and non-Euclidean geometry. His critique of Euclid’s congruency postulate in the Science of Logic organizes the distinction:
Thus Euclid’s first propositions about triangles deal only with congruence, that is, how many parts in a triangle must be determined, in order that the remaining parts of one and the same triangle, or the whole of it, shall be altogether determined. The comparison of two triangles with one another, and the basing of congruence on coincidence is a detour necessary to a method that is forced to employ sensuous coincidence instead of the thought, namely, the determinateness of the triangles.
There is a geometry (that, remember, is a level abstracted away from the determinations of space!) which involves the relation between the certainty of the understanding travelling through sensuousness. There is also a geometry which remains in the pure thought of the understanding. The former Stepelevich calls “physical” geometry, and the latter “pure” geometry. What the geometry of the pure understanding provides us with is the fact that the naturality of tridimensional space is taken to hold only for concrete apprehension, for image-thought, and not for the abstract reflections of pure geometry, nor for the determinations of space. An objection might occur here that one expects Hegel to celebrate the intuited three dimensions of space found in “physical geometry” as proof of his method, however, in actuality he rejects this, arguing that geometry is always a level abstracted away from the determinations of space, and, as a science, three-dimensionality itself is not derived from the concept (read: conceptual determinations) of space.
In so far as it is not a philosophical science, geometry may assume the universal determinations of space as its object, and it is not to be demanded of it that it should deduce the necessity of the three dimensions of space.
The differences we perceive between weight, length, and width, are themselves superficial and empty. We cannot define how they differ qualitatively because, at the level of pure thought, they are not yet actual and actualized differences, they are merely supposed to be different. From here, we may return to the establishment of a geometry with this in mind. Pure geometry is the science of abstract conceptual determinations. It deals with the “pure point” (akin to Hilbert’s formal points) which is ens rationis- an entity of reason. The point is once again the negation of space, that in its abstractness is not yet in intuitive space. This pure geometry then continues to describe the internal, necessary articulation of the concept of space as concept. Physical geometry is geometry as it relates to the natural world, to “actual physical mass”. The physical point is a here, a place. The point enters the realm of externality and is thus the “natural concretization” of the pure point. The dimensions (height, length, breadth) appear as the expression of physical mass occupying space. It is not the case that one is true, and the other is false. The culmination of the method in the enclosing surface and the restoration of the spatial totality corresponds to the space of physical bodies. This is a space that has returned to itself, a self-contained whole. This is also the space of “congruence” geometries (such as Euclidean, Spherical, Hyperbolic). These geometries, as Felix Klein systematized them, are concerned with spaces that are homogenous and have properties (such as invariance under translation and rotation), that make them suitable for describing a totality, a world-sphere in which figures can be moved without distortion. The pure mathematics are reflective of a geometry which takes the stages of point, line, and plane as pure moments, representing a space which has not yet achieved totality. They are abstract, one-sided determinations. The “pure point” is not yet in this space or that space. It is the abstract negation that could, in principle, generate any kind of spatial manifold. Both geometries are describing spatiality. Both are, in a sense, necessary. The error would be to mistake the science generated from the abstract moment as being inferior or superior to the science generated from the concrete moment.
Eva Brann’s monumental book, The World of the Imagination: Sum and Substance, offers a historical look at the domain of Euclidean space through an overview of imaginational reality. She inadvertently proves precisely what Hegel claims, that intuitive three-dimensionality is the form of apprehension, and not the content of the concept. Brann merely states it in the form of Euclidean geometry being the imagination’s “native idiom”, yet that theoretical cognition can and does transcend it. Both thinkers agree that Euclidean geometry is how we naturally perceive and imagine space, but it is not a logical necessity and does not exhaust the possibilities of spatiality, thought, and nature. The denial of the possibility of non-Euclidean geometry results from non-speculative thought; such a denial involves the philosophical failure to distinguish between intuition, or immediate imagination, and the concept’s free self-determination. When one asserts that Euclidean space is the only possible geometry, one is unconsciously elevating a report on the imagination’s structure into a limitation on being itself. This is, in essence, a Kantian position, albeit one Kant himself might not have fully endorsed, and one that Hegel’s speculative method definitively overcomes. Euler circles, Venn diagrams, Galilean parabolas, the armillary sphere of the Timaeus, all these are the imagination at work, rendering intelligible structures in intuitively accessible form.
I would like to return to the idea that I am not all invested in the enterprise of non-Euclidean geometry. However, truth-seeker that I am, it is hard to let statements which are blatantly false or mistaken stand, when appropriate to correct them. What I actually believe has very high overlap with Steiner— different geometries correspond to different modes of cognition/perceptions of space. Geometries all describe spatiality by very character of what they are. Geometry provides the quantitative structure of objects furnished from the Understanding’s position as the begetter of thought. As such, Euclidean geometry describes the space we perceive through the senses, it corresponds to waking sensory consciousness, being very accurate for the dead objects of the world; a world of fixed distances, rigid shapes, and so on and so forth. With mathematical thinking, in a geometric proof, Steiner states that “what is present in the mathematical as a conscious fact right up to the lines of reasoning is manageable and accompanied by inner experience from beginning to end”. This is the source of its certainty. The mathematical object is an image which geometry creates, a “pictorial” reality, and in relation to external nature, it is “unreality”. This is also to say that geometry and the determinations of space can never be wholly commensurate. The pure geometry of the understanding is precisely this realm of pictorial unreality. It is thought grasping pure form. So the Euclidean axioms, when modified by a Bolyai or ignored by a Lobachevsky, generate other geometries, they are generating other pictorial worlds from thought. This act of modification is the factual proof of the certainty one feels in the comprehensibility of mathematization. The mind can dwell in these different pictorial spaces with equal rigor and inner consistency.
If Euclidean geometry corresponds to our waking sensory consciousness, being the language for the physical world of mineral bodies, of forces that push and pull centrifugally, it is the geometry of the dead. For this domain, it is perfectly true. However, a “geometry of the living” is necessary. I propose projective geometry. The objects of projective geometry are not abstract objects, or dead objects. Its objects and mathematics describes the formative forces in the world; it is a geometry of process. Steiner himself pointed to the path from analytical to synthetic geometry as a path that leads out of the purely mathematical and towards an encounter with a differentiated, qualitative reality. As he argues, when you follow the implications of projective constructions, you are “forced out of the free formation of the mathematical and into something where the objective passes over into an inner determination, into an inner being”. You are, in essence, beginning to think into the living thing. It is for this reason that I do not put much stock into Euclidean or non-Euclidean geometry. Both have their place and purpose. I much prefer projective geometry for what it can help us visualize and intuit about the world. While more traditional exhibitions of projective geometry exist, such as Lawrence Edwards in The Vortex of Life: Nature’s Patterns in Space and Time, who creates computer generated models of plants, embryos, and organs, like the heart, Annie Besant and Charles Leadbeater’s Thought Forms offers the perfect opportunity for a spiritual-scientific geometry. In the book, they provide numerous illustrations and a clear typology of different forms. If Steiner is right that the respective geometry reflects different modes of cognition/perception of space, then projective geometry, with its focus on mobile, metamorphosing forms (like the conic sections found in Thought Forms), provides the mathematical language for these forces. A growing plant can be understood for the spiritual scientist as a metamorphosis of form that can be modeled by the continuous transformation of a projective curve. Additionally, Steiner’s “intuitive thinking” can help us comprehend the geometry of thought-forms as the visible trace of the etheric formative forces having been impressed upon by a soul quality. The qualitative, inner experience, and the living world of formative, spatial dynamics may provide us with a geometric phenomenon of the soul.
Any further arguments on the intrinsic character of space as “Euclidean” will be met by me with a demand to read Point and Line and Plane by Kandinsky. It is with a great disheartening mood that I must confess: arguments like these are not all that atypical for me when talking with Hegelians. In trying to convey meaning, one will be met with incredulity regarding structures and actualities outside the dogmatic form of the concept. The very argument mentioned has ceded ground to the dogma of the concept- I have not been a Hegelian for a while- and yet in all my efforts to explain why, using analogies and alternative ways of approach, the very approach of analogies and alternatives was the issue. Unless one already has doubts, which are the seeds of escape from the solipsism of the concept, communication will be rendered impossible with the interlocutor. Both thinking and being are restricted, disabling one from accepting that ultimately, we are all analogical. Everything we do and think is in an analogical relationship to the ultimate goodness, of Christ. Rejecting this reality of being and holding onto false Gods is nothing short of fanaticism. The possibility of self-criticism surely always needs to be present, and so my own position in these circumstances must be established. Words and ideas leave psychic imprints on me, such that I can almost “feel” the relationality of what is presented to me through thought. Responsibility must be taken in conveying what is “felt” or “seen” in this structure. However, the limit of accountability is the actions done by other people. In communication, if there is an inability to see what is there, you cannot force someone to see it, just as you cannot force a drug addict to see the good life. Likewise, an inability to afford grace in communication, that the other person is trying to communicate something, something more than their own failure of comprehension, denies the inner life present in all of us, through the grace of God.
Hamann’s Faith A confession must be made. The debates above reveal an archetype of sorts, of a person sealed inside a circle that has no outside, unable to receive what does not fit the predetermined form. What becomes clear in such moments is that argument alone cannot open what has been closed. Only a burgeoning from the inside may begin to crack the hardened shell of systems-thinking formalism. Something supplementary is needed, a therapy that targets the very aspiration to systemic totality which Hegel inherits from the German Idealist tradition and radicalizes. The person to offer such a therapy is the “Magus of the North”, Johann Georg Hamann.
I have spent years myself inside this circle of circles, I know it’s language, it’s satisfactions, I know it’s Euclidean centrality. And I know what it costs to leave, to slowly recognize that the circle, for all its solidarity, cannot contain what God provides. Relationality is felt, but grace arrives unbidden, a confession must be made:
There is no pure reason. Reason is never abstract, and never universal, nor self-standing. It always belongs to a particular planet, a particular zeitgeist, shaped by a particular language, and tradition. The Hegelian system, for example, for all its claims to absolute knowing, remains immensely humanist: it can only ever be a relationality that is intelligible to us, not to a relationality to the object itself. What sense would it make to speak of a relationality we have no relationship with? The very question lends us the answer.
We approach this limit constantly through analogy. The relationship between God and humanity, the divine and the human, is an analogical one, based on the free act of God in the Incarnation. The union of two natures in Christ is a mystery to be believed and adored, rather than something to be reconciled by speculative reason. The relationship is open because the kind of operative protocol the concept undergoes is incapable of getting from the human to the divine. There is a gap that can only be crossed by a gift from the other side. The attempt to expunge reason of its contingency is the proton pseudos of systematicity and the beginning of the neglect of faith. Reason’s purpose is to reveal its own limits. Like the law in Pauline theology, reason is a pedagogue. It leads us to the end of our own resources, exhausting its own resources until what is left is what is. Surely, Hegel’s methodology can be described as a series of self-exhaustions of the system itself. But for Hegel, this exhaustion leads to an ever higher integration, an apotheosis of reason. For Hamann, it leads elsewhere entirely. Reasons self-critique is meant to show us our ignorance and need for something beyond ourselves. When reason claims to be sufficient, it becomes idolatrous. Reason itself is a fallen creature, a fallen human capacity. It is given sensations, given a tradition, given a language, and ultimately given revelation. Its job is to acknowledge this dependence, not to spin everything out of itself. Thus, reason’s ultimate purpose is to lead us to the end of our own resources. It is meant to fail, to show us the abyss. This is the exact opposite of Hegel’s project, where reason’s self-examination leads to its own apotheosis. For Hamann, reason’s examination leads only to its own limit. What lies beyond that limit is not for reason to say.
Perhaps what lies beyond that limit, what is given is language. In the beginning was the Word, after all. Hamann has the view that language is prior to reason. To an Idealist, this would seem like a crude, empirical assertion which the concept would sublate (yes I will use that word). They would almost immediately assert: “Prior? prior in what sense? Chronologically? Empirically? This would not make sense, given it would be anthropological, rather than philosophical. Spirit sublates such empirical conditions into its own self-conscious logical development. The truth of language is the Concept!”.
Hamann is certainly not making a claim about cavemen grunting before being able to think. He is making a humbler claim about the nature of all thought- that language is the inescapable medium of all thought. You cannot get behind it or beyond it to a purer realm of thought, whether that realm is Kant’s transcendental unity of apperception, or Hegel’s pure self-thinking thought. The idea of a thought that has fully sublated its linguistic expression into a pure, self-present meaning is an illusion. Steiner himself has commented on how a spiritual person like Hamann would be quick to jump against the position that meaning is not immanent, or located within some “outside”. He would likely say to Hegel’s Concept, if one were to hold the position that the form is “sublated” (and in some sense contingent to the word, whether it be the rational natural contingency of form, or the formalism of the Understanding which only values symbolic coherence) that for all its claimed concreteness it is just another attempt to vault over the abyss of language’s being, which is itself being.
Hamann often uses the term “hysteron proteron” to accuse both Kant, and by extension, any idealist system of getting things backwards. Such a position would treat the a priori (the logical, conceptual) as if it were the foundation, when in fact it is derived from and always dependent upon the a posteriori. The genealogical priority of language means that what philosophy claims is first (the Concept) is actually “last”. Or as scripture puts it: so the last shall be first, and the first last. The very possibility of an abstract logical determination purifying itself of its linguistic and embodied skin is denied by Hamann. There is no thought before or outside language. There is only thought in language, shaped by it and carried by it. Consider a practical example. What is gender? One approach might attempt a rational determination: gender is logically rooted in the sexed distinction, a clean conceptual definition which admits no variability (although it certainly leaves room for it). This approach, for all its claim to necessity, tells you nothing about what gender actually is! It is indistinguishable from a dogmatic traditionalism that simply asserts the union of gender and sex. The alternative is to attend to how gender appears through particular cultures, how it is lived, and spoken, and named. This second approach does not begin with the concept and apply it to the world. It begins with the world, with language and tradition, and lets the concept emerge from there. The concept, in this case, is not the foundation, but the result. It is last, not first. This is essentially Hamann’s point. Language is not conceived of as the clothing of the thought, it is its body. You cannot strip it away to find the pure concept underneath, because there is nothing underneath. The concept is not behind the word, it is in the word, inseparable from it, given with it. And this is why reason, which thought itself sovereign, must confess its dependence. Reason does not invent language for itself, it is born into language, it relates the word to itself. It does not master language, it is mastered by it. Reason is pedagogue to Christ. The ultimate purpose of reason and its self-critique is to lead us to the end of our own resources. It is meant to fail, to show us the “abyss,” so that we might stop trusting in it and instead trust in the Word that is given to us. This is the exact opposite of Hegel’s project, where reason’s self-examination leads to its own apotheosis, what Steiner calls logical fetishism (for more reasons than this).
There are some relations you have to take on faith, relations which reason itself cannot immediately show you. For example, it is inconceivable to see karmic links between people within a framework like Kant’s or Hegel’s. The Incarnation is another example. In Christ, divine and human natures unite without fusion, without one side being reduced to the other. You cannot arrive at the self-relationality of Christ by reasoning, because this system of relationality is not exhaustive of Christ’s self-relationality, it is only exhaustive of the intelligibility reason has. This is why faith is not a substitute for reason, nor a moment to be subsumed within reason’s development. Faith is the mode of relating to what exceeds us- to the God who gives himself, to the Word that speaks to us, and to the relations that hold us together, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Faith is the trust that the gift is good, and that the giver is faithful. In the future this mode of relation will develop itself into a new faculty. Nevertheless, this will be for a future time, brought along by a revolution as paradigm shifting as non-Euclidean geometry was for the Kantians.
title: Hamann and the Non-Euclidean Revolution of Faith

content:
Of all the human faculties, language is perhaps the most intimate and mysterious, being responsible for conjuring emotions in the soul, and drawing out latent potential, all by an invocation of symbols and signs that we use daily. In its original and true nature, language was Logos, the divine creative principle that ordered the cosmos. The Word was understood in the past as an active power, a vibration that participated directly in the essence of what it named. There existed no arbitrary divide between the signifier and the signified, sound and being been fused. To utter a true name was therefore not to label a thing from the outside, but to invoke its reality and align with its spiritual core. This ontological unity rendered language inherently sacramental. Blessings, curses, and ritual incantations were seen as having immediate metaphysical effects because speech was an act of cosmic participation, distinct from its conception today as mere formality or social convention. This sanctity gave rise to the concept of holy languages. Traditions revered Sanskrit, Hebrew, Examination Chinese, Quranic Arabic, Biblical Latin, and so on and so forth, as Marshall McLuhan rightfully commented, as revealed architectures, or novel vessels for truth. To think, pray, or compose in these languages was to operate within a (and the) divine framework, the only medium capable of housing certain transcendental realities. Furthermore, this unity was experienced sensibly. The resonance of a word was organically tied to its meaning. Phenomena like the Bouba/Kiki effect (where people intuitively associate rounded sounds with rounded shapes and vice versa) are thus interpreted as the surviving remnants of this primordial unity. They are fleeting proof that the human soul retains a memory of a time when form and essence, or sound and sense, were one.
An ancient view this is, if for the fact that it casts our contemporary experience of language into a puzzling relief. For, we do not live in a world of true names and efficacious words. We inhabit a universe of arbitrary signs, where cat could just as easily be “gato”, “neko”, or “Katze”, as a matter of social agreement and contingency (or in some cases a view which deprives language of spiritual significance but has to concede that it fits mechanically in one way or another) rather than an explicit connection with cosmic truth. There is not a discrepancy, this in fact evidences a great decay from language as a participatory event, into convention. The fall from logos is the story of language becoming unmoored from being, a process intrinsically linked to the evolution of consciousness itself. This shift is a necessary, if not arduous, stage in Spirit’s journey. It corresponds to the epochal awakening of the Consciousness Soul - the modern principle of self-reflective individualism. To achieve self-awareness and freedom, human consciousness had to turn decisively inward, withdrawing its projective participation from the world. This inward turn, mirroring modernity’s deterritorializing force, required the very severing of the ancient bond between word and thing. Language had to be drained of its innate albeit given meaning, so that the sovereign “I” could become capable of grasping meaning from within as well as without. The celebrated arbitrariness of the sign, therefore, is in a way a diagnostic mark of this age. What linguistics posits as a neutral principle is the very mechanism of alienation required for the birth of the modern ego. The word becomes an empty shell, a detached signifier, because the conscious self needed a neutral tool to wield, rather than a sacred power to which it was subject.
The historical indices of this fall are certainly tangible. The evolution from pictographic to phonetic writing is a tell-tale sign. Hieroglyphs and ideograms maintained an iconic, mimetic link to the world. For example, a picture of a sun still carried a trace of the sun’s essence. The alphabet, a triumph of abstraction, reduces speech to a code of neutral symbols representing pure sound. This form of language is the perfect technology (in the sense of techne) for the Consciousness Soul, seeing as it is analytic efficient, and severed from iconic presence. In addition to this, it enables the dissemination of abstract thought, but at the same time finalizes the divorce of the written word from the living cosmos. This abstraction was compounded by the “scientific” and nominalist worldviews, which exiled qualities and essences from the material world, leaving only quantities and names. Language, in this world, can only point to surfaces, and likewise the commonality which etymology sheds light on can only reasonably signify a correlation of concept with the convenience of spatiotemporal form. Furthermore, the Bouba/Kiki effect now appears in a tragic light as a testament to what was lost. It tells of old modes of consciousness and participatory magic (and language, back then, was by all means magic) flaring up in a mind otherwise conditioned to see sound and shape as separate domains. It is the soul’s muted protest against its own conceptual imprisonment.
Thus, the modern condition of language as a formal system, as a convenient but hollow geometry for the ego’s use, is the subjective reality of this objective historical stage. The feeling that language is an internal combinatory space is not its eternal truth, it is only what is left after the spirit of Logos has withdrawn. However, it is not all dire. The great emptying of meaning in the world, can also be considered a great preparation. It is the necessary negative moment in the process of totality. By passing through this desert of abstraction, by experiencing language as a detached and formal medium, the Consciousness Soul gains the unprecedented capacity for critical self-reflection and free conceptual recognition. It must lose the given, participatory Word, to eventually find it again, consciously and from within. The arbitrary language of the modern age is therefore the condition in which the possibility of a future redeemed speech is created. Having fully integrated the power of abstract thought, humanity will one day speak with the authority of Logos.
For now, we have to deal with what Heidegger called “Idle Talk”. The ultimate manifestation of this emptied, circulating language, is what Heidegger characterizes as speech that has completely lost its anchor in the primordial, disclosive power of Logos. It is language reduced to a self-referential social mechanism. Words are passed along not to reveal the truth of a matter, but precisely to avoid encountering it. In Idle Talk, understanding/substance (they are etymologically the same word, fun fact!) is simulated through the effortless absorption of pre-digested and “average” meanings. The reality of the arbitrary sign is a closed circuit of chatter where words refer only to other words, creating a comforting shallow consensus that shields the modern ego from the demanding world-disclosing responsibility of authentic speech. This is the sacred Word that once invoked being, having devolved, in its most common social form, into a background noise of cliché and hearsay- it is the sound of consciousness speaking to itself from within itself.
A descent into abstraction needs a habitat, and it finds this habitat in the digital realm of the internet, the perfect expression of modernity’s de-temporalized, deterritorialized space. Here, the inward-turning Consciousness Soul constructs a world in its own image featuring a realm of pure information where language exists in a state of frictionless circulation, liberated not only from the object, but from the being and body of the Earth. The internet is the ecosystem in which idle talk becomes the dominant, self-replicating mode of being, setting the state for a platform like TikTok to emerge as the apotheosis of post-literate communication.
The linguistic ecology of TikTok represents the logical result of this trajectory in the form of the metastasis of Idle Talk into an optimized and fully realized system. The platform’s hallmarks of gratuitous abbreviation, intentional misspellings, syntactic compression, and the rapid, vampiric repurposing of words (particularly from African American Vernacular English) into mimetic signals are the signature of language accelerating into pure social functionality, shedding any remaining pretensions to depth, history, or disclosure. When “because” becomes “bc”, or “this shit” becomes “ts”, or complex (in form, not in substance) statements are cramped into acronyms, we witness the final abstraction of the sign. The word lacks being even a full shell- it is fragment. It is a token whose value is determined solely by its velocity and adhesive power within the circuit of communal recognition.
This is Idle Talk weaponized and accelerated by the habitat of the internet. Heidegger’s concept feared a chatter that levels all understanding to the average, whereas TikTok’s architecture actively enforces it. Algorithmic virality rewards surface level novelty and in-group fluency, incentivizing the continuous production of linguistic shortcuts that bypass conscious reflection by the subject, in order to trigger instant affective or tribal response. The intentional “wrongness” of a spelling is a performative rejection of external authority, creating a closed vernacular that fortifies community boundaries on the one hand, and further divorces the sign from any stable referent on the other. “Rizz” and “sigma” are not in a dictionary (yet, as far as I know), but still exists in the collective performance of its use as a purely circulating social value.
In a sense, we can call this beyond illiteracy (although America has a major illiteracy problem, and you’d expect nothing less from a democratic nation), into post-literacy, defined as the linguistic state perfectly adapted to the Consciousness Soul’s internalization. Language has become a pure tool of the sovereign ego, constructing transient identities and affiliations in a digital das Man. However, even within this schema, even in the apparent decay, a purpose is served. TikTok language, as the extreme of Idle Talk, makes the alienation of the sign total and unmistakable. By reducing communication to its most fluid and disposable form, it forces a kind of crisis. One either surrenders to endless circulation for its own sake, or begins to feel the hunger for a Word that means. In this sense, the vernacular of the platform serves as a negative positivity. It is the resonant sound of the hollow Logos, waiting to be filed again by a spirit that has fully passed through the task of its own grasping, waiting to be recognized from within and from without.
title: Idle Talk in the Age of TikTok: On Linguistic Decay

content:
Intro Listening to JID’s song “K-Word” off the album “God Does Like Ugly” was what inspired this article. The album revolves around who is worthy of God’s love, an inversion of the American hyperreality which proclaims that God loves the “hardworking” middle class that can do no wrong. The stereotypical image presented in America is almost comical; that of the white picket fence, lack of personal and community problems, ease of accessibility to wants and needs and so forth. Instead, JID claims that no— God loves the downtrodden, he loves the suffering, the lost, the confused. Because if he doesn’t, then who will? The underbelly of America is multi-faceted, however one of the few populations that have been consistently neglected are the American Descendants of Slavery. No, Robin DiAngelo’s White Fragility has not helped this population, neither did the half-white half-Kenyan president with likely familial CIA connections, nor has the influx of non-American black migrants which move here with an intact family structure, culture, etc., been indicative of change. JID essentially says that God Does Like Ugly, the inversion of the southern Black American saying “God don’t like ugly”- a judgment on the inevitable justice one will receive through karma, whether through retribution or salvation. The project is a theological inversion of American moral mythology, among many things. No more is this apparent than on the song “K-Word”, with the title clearly being an allusion to karma. Throughout the song, karma is personified as a woman, being responsible for anger, crimes, and temptation (the temptation to blame your situation on karma). Eventually, JID concludes that if he reconciles karma, he can end “the cycle” because he has seen the light. The cycle mentioned here refers to self-reinforcing loops of trauma via gang violence, retaliation, crime, and in general the fatalistic thinking which persists across individuals and generations. The song has a tri-part message it is rejecting: prosperity theology, where success is virtue, karmic determinism where suffering is deserved, and the kind of social nihilism which says that nothing can change. Instead, it proposes that grace exists precisely where the system declares there is none, and that you will get what you deserve in the end.
In light of this, many people wonder about karma, and more generally, about the law which is responsible for the things in their life. Karma is individual account of a persons’ spiritual and moral history. It is the sum total of everything a soul has done, thought, and felt, which is then transformed and carried forward. Such an account is the raw material, that between lives, is metamorphosed into the forces that shape the next life. A thought or impulse becomes a predisposition for a specific bodily organ or life circumstance. A core definition of karma is the process by which inner, spiritual qualities become outer, physical destiny. This happens in the period between death and a new birth. For example, the sex of a person in one life is the karmic result of the soul’s experiences in the previous life. A woman’s deeply emotional and psychic experiences become the organizing force for a male body in the next incarnation, and vice versa. Similarly, a tendency toward error can, after death, be transformed into a damaged organ in the next life. At the most fundamental level, karma is the process by which the two basic elements of earthly existence - Light (matter) and Love (soul) - are harmonized together, often imperfectly, through the actions of the Luciferic and Ahrimanic beings. Luciferic beings are interested in weaving light into love. When this happens, the soul (love) becomes permeated with a Luciferic element (such as pride or egotism). This impure love then impregnates the physical body (light). This damages the “light” substance of the body, creating a darkness within it. This is what damages the physical manifestation of karma, or what we experience as illness of organic weakness. Therefore, karma is the record of this interweaving. It is the consequence of how the soul has shaped and been shaped by its encounters with the world (light), under the influence of Lucifer and Ahriman.
Luciferic influence on the karmic process acts from within. This influence temps man through egotism, pride, vanity, ambition, greed, any manifestation of the inflation of the Ego. These are passions that when we succumb, we create a cause that is transformed into a tendency for illness in the next life. Ahrimanic influence acts from without. It is only made possible because Luciferic influence has already created a disorder in the human being, distorting our perception of the external world. Ahriman then insinuates himself into this inaccurate picture, leading to error and materialism. His influence leads to organic damage that may not be accompanied by pain. The two forces are always interacting.
Given this picture, every person is ultimately responsible for themselves, for not falling into sin. It might seem quite individualistic at first, but we will explore this later. Before that exposition, an elaboration on the possibility of justice is necessary. Justice in this process is guided by beneficent powers that counteract Lucifer and Ahriman. When an illness results from a past Luciferic failing, benevolent powers add pain to the illness. Pain serves as a subconscious teacher, educating us so we will not repeat the same mistake. Pain can be considered a gift that allows one to escape the clutches of Luciferic temptation. When an Ahrimanic influence damages an organ (precisely by causing the etheric body to penetrate it too deeply, binding us a little too close to the physical world. Lucifer works on the astral, Ahriman on the etheric), benevolent forces step in to destroy or paralyze that organ. This prevents us from becoming hopelessly entangled in Maya, allowing our spirit to find its way back to the spiritual world. The destruction of the organ is always a saving grace. Divine justice on the individual level is an inherent, corrective wisdom implicit in the process and the cosmos, working through these experiences to help guide evolution on the right track.
Why do bad people succeed? This is among the most difficult questions which gnaw at the curiosity of man. Steiner explicitly describes karma as a life-account where good and bad are tallied. The thing to remember is that this account is only balanced over multiple lifetimes, not within one. If a person, such as Bill Gates, is “bad” and successful, it means their current life is being lived on the surplus of good deeds from a past life. Their current wrongdoings are creating a new deficit that will have to be balanced in a future life. The apparent success is the interest on an old karmic deposit, and not a reward for current behavior. It is not a blank check either, it might just be one that they can’t cash. Steiner also emphasizes that the consequences of an action do not travel directly from one life to the next as the same thing. They are transformed during the long period between death and a new birth. A wrongdoing in one life (such as an act of egotism inspired by Lucifer, or the invocation of black magic) does not cause a punishment. Instead, it is metamorphosed into a tendency, or predisposition, for a specific life circumstance or illness in the next life. This transformation takes time. Therefore, the effect is not immediate in the next life, as if it is a cause-effect relationship. It is already reconciled in the substance of the person’s new body and destiny before they are even born. We see the person’s “success”, but we do not see the organic weaknesses or karmic traps they have woven into their own future.
A point which prevents fatalism is that the persons’ life is not only the result of past actions. New, primary actions can enter.
“Those things which are due to happen in the course of human evolution must come about through human agency and that these individual agents have to be brought out of the spiritual world, without consideration whether they are fully ready in themselves. They are born for the purposes of human evolution, and a karmic path has to be interrupted or lengthened… In such cases a destiny is thrust upon men which need have no relation to their past karma.”
A “bad” person might be successful because they are an instrument for a larger historical or evolutionary purpose (like Charlemagne [Harun al Rashid who was concurrent with him was better], Aristotle, or Luther). Their personal karmic debt is temporarily set aside so they can fulfill a task for the general karma of humanity.
Often times, the consequences of wrongdoing are not external misfortunes we can see. They work in the subconscious and in the structure of that persons being. A person who has committed wrong may carry within them a damaged etheric body, or a tendency toward a specific illness that has not yet manifested (or perhaps they are fully depraved and want to become the illness such as Prince Phillip). Or they may suffer from a great emptiness. When external life becomes too comfortable (as it does for some “successful” people), the soul experiences a void and rushes from sensation to sensation trying to fill it. This inner hollowness is a form of suffering invisible to an outside observer.
Karmic Relationships Some traditions say that incarnation is a result of being caught in a karmic trap, forcing the higher soul to incarnate on the physical world. There is some truth to this. However, Steiner offers a slightly different view, insofar as the conditions under which we incarnate are reconciled with the fact that we do not incarnate in isolation. Souls are bound together through shared experiences across many lives, forming what he calls “karmic groups”. This means that the people in your life, your family, friends, enemies, even brief acquaintances, are likely souls you have traveled with before. The force which draws you together again is the need for karmic adjustment, to balance debts or fulfill connections created in the past.
“What is really guiding this wiser man in us when he wants to meet this or that person? … we want to meet him because we have met him previously. It may not have happened in the last life; it could have happened much earlier. The wiser man in us leads us to this person because we had dealings with him in a previous life, or because we may have incurred a debt in one way or another. We are led to this person as though by magic.”
There is an aspect, a part of our being, active below the threshold of consciousness, that guides us toward the people we need to meet. This “wiser man” works with the forces inscribed in our astral body during the Kamaloca period after death. When we wrong someone, we must experience their pain in that state, and this experience imprints a force that later draws us back to them for resolution. Steiner gives us a remarkable set of rules for when in our lives we are most likely to meet people from past incarnations. These are based on the “climax and pivoting point” around our thirties. The first is from birth to ~30 years. We meet with people whom we were connected during the middle period of a previous life. This is why parents, siblings, and childhood playmates are often souls we knew intimately in a past life, at a different stage. The second period is in the “middle” of life, from ~30 to ~50 years of age. We meet with whom we were connected during the early childhood of a previous life. This is also the time when we may encounter those who were our parents in a past life. The second half of life are more complex, it does not follow a static pattern. Meetings may happen with people we were connected with before, but often the karma is too extensive to be resolved in one life. These meetings can begin a process that will span future incarnations.
Recognizing Karmic Connection Steiner distinguishes between two different kinds of encounters, which you can identify by the nature of the impression made. The first type is based on the will and the heart. It’s a very deep, immediate pull. You feel that you must follow or oppose the person, regardless of their external appearance or intellect. It may manifest as love, hate, or an intense sense of being drawn to people. When discussing “soul ties” and “soul partners” (a topic we will not have time to go over), often times, only a positive affinity is discussed, but the possibility of a negative affinity, that two people, maybe who love each other, drive each other to destruction is never considered. The karmic significance of this first type indicates a strong karmic connection from a past life. The will is working before the meeting even takes places, guided by the lunar forces of necessity. It’s not too crazy to think of this in romantic terms either— that the significant other you were immediately drawn to was fated to be met; that all your life lead up to the moment you have met them. However romantic this is, this notion does discount the freedom that we have.
The second type of karmic connection is based on the intellect and the aesthetic. Admittedly, this is a more superficial judgment. You may admire the person, find them interesting, a quite “splendid fellow”. But they do not affect your deepest will or inner being. All this indicates is that there is no strong past karmic bond. This is a new meeting, brought about by solar forces, which can become the basis for future karma.
“If the effect made by the two upon each other reaches into the will, into the heart, into the inmost nature, then a karmic connection exists… If an effect made by another person reaches only into the intellect, into the aesthetic sense, this is not an outcome of the Moon’s activity, but a situation brought about by the Sun and one that will have its sequel only in the future.”
Karmic ties of non-individuals: Humanity and Cultures I previously mentioned the “general karma of humanity”. What is meant here is that there is an overarching plan of human evolution. Great historical events, like the Persian Wars, cannot be explained by the individual karma of the participants alone. They serve a purpose for the whole of humanity, a continuous stream of civilization guided by normally progressing spiritual hierarchies. A concept known as “impulse groups” is also important here. This concept specifies specific collections of individualities who incarnate together in a particular time and place to generate a major cultural or spiritual impulse. An example can be considered the Florentine Renaissance Group from the 15th century wherein a network of souls around the Medici family, including Ficino, Pico della Mirandola, Botticelli, da Vinci, Michaelangelo, and Raphael incarnated together to foster a rebirth of Platonic and artistic impulses in Europe. The German Idealist group including Goethe, Schiller, Novalis, Hegel, Fichte, and Schelling is another example. Such groups show that entire cultural epochs are the karmic project of specific soul communities, working together over centuries to advance human consciousness.
Collective/group karma may also express itself through the life of a folk soul or national spirit. An individual incarnates within a particular folk soul for a single lifetime, but the folk soul itself is a distinct higher spiritual being with its own evolution.
“To identify oneself with a folk soul is, in reality, totally devoid of meaning… We shall only understand these things properly, once we can look earnestly and deeply at the truths of reincarnation and karma.”
Additionally, the specific bloodlines and family lineages into which we are born are actively prepared by us in the spiritual world between deaths. For centuries before our birth, we are immersed in the forces of a particular family stream, working to bring about the exact physical and social circumstances we need.
Having now the distinction between individual and group karma (and furthermore, that of the whole karma of humanity), we can understand aberrations in the “divine plan”. Successive civilizations arise and flourish, each driven by a necessary “illusion” that its own culture is eternal. This enthusiasm, which allows the culture to make its unique contribution to humanity, is the work of Luciferic spirits, who enkindle this fervor into human astral bodies. A civilization’s decline and its treasures being passed on is the work of Ahriman, who “spoils” what Lucifer has created. From this perspective, a social system like the caste system could be understood as a concretization of the perceived spiritual laws of karma and reincarnation, within the “general karma” of a particular cultural epoch. It would be the form in which that culture’s understanding of cosmic justice was lived out, driven by the necessary “illusion” and enthusiasm of its time. The justified position of a Dalit class in India, in this view, would be a misunderstanding, or a hardened, materialist (Ahrimanic) interpretation of a truth about karm/reincarnation, which originally was meant to explain individual growth and freedom, not to eternally fix a social hierarchy. Steiner continuously emphasizes that karma is not “fate” and that we cannot simply say someone’s current misfortune is the karmic result of a past life without knowing the full context, which includes forward-looking purposes as well.
Animal Karma Animals do not have individual karma, in the same way that humans do. Their experiences of life, death, and destiny are governed by a different principle. The main distinction lies in the nature of the soul. Man possesses an individual Ego that reincarnates and carries its karmic account from life to life. Animals, on the other hand, are part of a group soul or generic soul. The group soul is a higher, collective spiritual being that oversees a whole species of group of animals. The individual animal is like a temporary expression or limb of this group soul. Steiner provides an analogy to explain how the group soul experiences the death of an individual animal. It is not a moment of individual destiny, as it is for a human.
“For the group soul the death of an animal resembles somewhat our experience at the approach of summer, when we have our hair cut shorter, which will then slowly grow again. The group soul of a species feels the death of an animal like the death of a limb which will gradually be replaced.”
Because the individual animal is not the permanent bearer of an Ego, it cannot generate its own karma or carry consequences from one physical life to the next. The karmic responsibility and evolution occur at the level of the group soul. The group soul is the entity that evolves, learns, and experiences the consequences of the collective actions of its members across time. The individual animal may be considered a fleeting manifestation of that collective being.
Although the external events of birth and death look similar for humans and animals, the inner experience is completely different. For a human, birth is a descent into individual consciousness, and death is a gateway to the spiritual world, where the Ego reviews its past life and prepares for the next. For an animal, birth and death are simply the emergence and dissolution of a temporary physical form for the enduring group soul.
Destiny, The Question of Freedom Suffering can be a trial, or even a mystery unrelated to personal wrongdoing. There are things beyond individual karma, this is what the Book of Job is partly about. Not everything about a person can be traced back to their own past deeds, this is a contingency of life. Steiner himself makes this point below:
“Take the case of Luther. We cannot just simply ascribe everything he experienced and endured to his karma; we must be clear that those things which are due to happen in the course of human evolution must come about through human agency and that these individual agents have to be brought out of the spiritual world, without consideration whether they are fully ready in themselves. They are born for the purposes of human evolution, and a karmic path has to be interrupted or lengthened, so that the individuality concerned may appear at a certain time. In such cases a destiny is thrust upon men which need have no relation to their past karma. “
Job finds a direct parallel. His suffering is not presented as a punishment for his sins, it is a “destiny thrust upon him”, because his soul was chosen to play a role in a test of faithfulness that serves a purpose beyond his individual karmic account. Steiner gives another concrete example that echoes this principle:
“Let us take a case which actually happened. In a natural catastrophe a number of people perished. It is not at all necessary to believe that it was in their karma that they all should thus perish together; this would be a cheap supposition. … There is an instance that has been investigated of a number of people perishing in an elemental catastrophe which resulted in a close alliance of these people at a later period, and, owing to their common fate, they gained the strength to undertake something in common. Through this catastrophe they were able to turn from materialism and brought with them in their next incarnation a disposition to spirituality.”
In the example above, the catastrophe was certainly not a byproduct of wrongdoing brought by karma. It was an event that came from the general karma of humanity to create a new impulse for the future.
While events can be beyond an individual person’s karma, these events are not placed in the realm of pure chance, or some inscrutable divine will separate from the law of karma. Steiner expands upon the concept of karma to include the actions and destinies of the gods. The entire 11th lecture of “Manifestations of Karma” (GA 120) is dedicated to this notion. The suffering and struggles of humanity are interwoven from the disappointments and sacrifices of higher beings. From this perspective, an event like Job’s trial is not “beyond karma” in the sense of being a lawless plaything of some God or another. It is part of a larger karmic system involving spiritual beings. Job’s suffering serves a purpose in a dialogue between divine and adversarial powers, karmic interactions at a level far above the human. Steiner even describes the great disappointment of the Luciferic spirits as they see their work constantly destroyed by Ahriman. This is their karma. Man, by consciously developing love and wisdom, will one day be able to redeem these higher beings, repaying the karmic debt for the gifts of freedom they bestowed.
The question still needs to be explored: how is karma not fate? How is it not deterministic? Under what circumstances can one say that freedom is an actuality, an actual achievement and not an illusion? The answer is deceivingly simple. Freedom is the goal of the entire karmic process, made possible by the tension between necessity and conscious human development. If karma were fate, your current life would be nothing but the inescapable result of the past. But because new causes are constantly being created in the present, the future remains open. The past provides the conditions and tendencies, but it does not dictate every choice. The buck eventually has to stop at you and your independence.
Paradoxically, the possibility of human freedom is directly attributed to Luciferic influence. Without it, humans would have evolved as passive recipients of divine gifts, unable to make independent choices. Lucifer has provided us with the gift of discrimination. The Luciferic beings, by tempting humans with the full range of passions, gave humanity the gift of choice. They introduced the very possibility of error, but also the possibility by consciously choosing the good. This capacity for oscillation between good and evil is the very ground in which a free, self-aware “I” can develop.
Previously, in the section on “recognizing karmic connection”, mention was made of lunar and solar forces. These are symbolic representations of how necessity and freedom interweave in our lives. Lunar forces govern everything that leads up to a significant event, such as a meeting between two people. Before they meet, their paths are guided by an “iron necessity” stemming from past karma. This is the work of the Moon beings, who have inscribed past experiences into the astral body, creating an unconscious drive that leads two people to the same point in time and space.
“what has been in operation between two human beings before they actually meet in earthly life is determined by the Moon… what occurs between two human beings before they become acquainted can only be regarded as the outcome of iron necessity…”
Sun/solar forces govern everything that happens after the meeting. Once the individuals are face to face, the past has fulfilled its purpose. The present moment, with its conscious choices and impressions, now becomes the scaffolding by which freedom can build or destroy itself. This is the work of the Sun beings.
”…everything that takes place between them after their meeting is determined by the Sun… as the expression of freedom, of mutually free relationship and behaviour.”
The meeting point itself is where the thread of past karma is united with the thread of future karma. What we do with the encounter, how we act, what we choose, that creates the new causes that will shape our future destiny.
If freedom is the goal, how then do we achieve it? Well, that’s the question, isn’t it…
That is a question I refuse to answer, no matter how many times it is brought up. Self-awareness is the only prerequisite. In the future, human evolution of consciousness will have a setting where freedom will be enhanced because we will be able to see karma directly, supposedly. This capacity may even begin to emerge before the middle of the 20th century, according to Steiner. When a person can see, after an action, a picture of what they still owe to balance the scales, their choices become far more conscious and therefore far more free. Acting with blind impulse no longer becomes an option, a clear vision of consequences is available.
On a final note, it is worth realizing that human freedom is not just for our own benefit. Our conscious development of love and wisdom is destined to redeem the very beings who made our freedom possible (Lucifer and Ahriman). Valentin Tomberg has once formulated that the two thieves on the cross represent Lucifer and Ahriman- the one who asks Christ to be remembered, receiving a promise of paradise, was Lucifer. The one who mocked Christ was Ahriman. Thieves indeed, for they have stolen a piece of divinity as Prometheus did fire, Lucifer who enkindled our passions and gave us the capacity for free choice, suffers continual disappointment as his work is destroyed by Ahriman. In the future, humanity will consciously and alchemically transmute Lucifer’s gift of passionate desire into selfless love. We will transform Ahriman’s realm of domain and error and illusion into wisdom. By doing so, we will repay our karmic debt to these higher beings and help them evolve.
title: Karma's a $@&%*!

content:
To be or not to be (African)? The recent AFCON 2025 win by Senegal (versus Morocco) has brought to mind an inflammatory question, or rather an observation, which casts doubt on the nature of “North Africa” in the context of being a part of Africa. North Africa’s identity is the site of a fundamental antagonism wherein objectively it is an inescapable part of the African continent by every material measure (such as geologically it is located on the continent, and it’s history, which includes its political economy and historical trade routes and such). At the same time subjectively, its people’s self-determined identity is overwhelmingly anchored in the MENA (Middle East and North Africa) civilizational sphere, often defined in explicit contrast to “Africa”. This contradiction doesn’t strike me as a mistake to be solved, but the central dogma of the region’s modern condition. The very passion by which it disidentifies itself is proof of an unsettled relationship, rather than lack thereof. I am speaking, of course, of the anti-Blackness present in North Africa.
Ask a Moroccan or an Algerian if they are “African”, and see the varied response one will be met with. The answer, when and if it comes, is rarely a simple yes, barre the cases when conceding the very attachment of geography isn’t the framing of the question (so, answers like “of course it’s African, it is in Africa!”). “African”, if used at all, is often appended as a geographical afterthought- “we are in Africa, but we are not of it”. This is the crux of what can be called a painful schism. On the one side lies the objective and unchangeable fact of the being. On the other, lies the subjective force of self-conception which aligns itself historically, culturally, and religiously, with the Middle East and the Mediterranean; an alignment with the Arab-Islamic world of the MENA region. In a way, North Africa inherits Europe’s defining mode of metaphysics and interface with the cosmos as “a forgetting of Being”, while at the same time being rooted differently, or at least partially conditioned by, Africa. Clearly, this goes beyond syntactics and into semantics. To dismiss it with the pussyfooted stance some may call diplomatic, that North Africa is simply “both” is a cheap cop-out. It throws dirt on the actual self-identity of millions for whom “African” is not a badge of belonging, but an indication of a history where distance was constructed through centuries of civilization and trade.
Before we can ask if North Africa is a part of Africa, we must first as: What do we even mean by Africa? The continent’s very name is an external imposition. To the ancient Greeks and Romans, the land south of the Mediterranean was not “Africa” but Libya, a coastal strip distinct from the interior. The term Africa likely stems from the Roman designation “Africa Proconsularis” for their province around Carthage, and was gradually expanded by European cartographers and colonizers. By the 19th century Scramble for Africa, it solidified as the label for the continent. In this sense, it’s very self-conception is wrought with externality. However, the case can be made that the imposed frame was reclaimed from within via the anti-colonial and Pan-African movements of the 20th century, led by Ghana’s Kwame Nkrumah, and Tanzania’s Julius Nyerere as a rallying cry for solidarity and shared destiny. This was further institutionalized through the founding of the Organization of African Unity. Here, “Africa” has become an idea of unity against oppression (but still externally determined). Despite this, in popular vernacular a kind of reductionism persists. Globally, and importantly in some cases within North Africa itself, the term “African” has been racially narrowed to a metonym for sub-Saharan Africa. It carries a connotation of Blackness, and a perceived cultural sphere separate from the Mediterranean and Arab worlds. This reduction is a trap which instantly ensnares our initial question, making “Are you African?” feel, to many North Africans, like being asked “Are you Black, or are you from that other Africa?”. The question of “Africa’s” determination has yet to be proven, and has only been theorized as of thus far. The true test of it as a distinct being will only be proven in the actualized existence of its people. The more focused question one can ask then is “what is North Africa, such that it brings about distinction from Africa ‘proper’?”. To answer this question, it is necessary to point out a fundamental divide within the Arab-Islamic world itself: the Maghreb (الْمَغْرِب‎ - “the West,” encompassing Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya, and often Mauritania) versus the Mashriq (الْمَشْرِق‎ - “the East,” the Arab states of the Middle East). A North African’s primary civilizational orientation is often first to this Maghreb-Mashriq continuum, not to a pan-African one.
Beneath this level lies the substance of the Amazigh, or Berber, people. These are the indigenous peoples of North Africa, with languages and cultures stretching back millennia before Phoenician or Arab arrival, and are the uncontested “African” layer of the region. To acknowledge the Amazigh is to acknowledge that North Africa’s deepest roots are autonomously and uniquely African, and not imported from the Middle East.
