This complicates any simple Arab vs Africa binary, and is key to understanding the regions’ character. Additionally, geography dictates an exception. The Nile Valley (primarily in Egypt and Sudan) forms a distinct corridor. Egypt’s history and psyche are fundamentally shaped by the river flowing from the African heartland. Sudan, even more explicitly, is a historical pivot point, a vast country torn by conflicts often framed as Arab versus African. This corridor ties North-Eastern Africa inextricably to the Sahel and the Horn, blurring any neat boundary and reminding us that the Sahara’s edges are zones of gradation and not distinct hard lined walls. Thus, we begin with contested definitions with “Africa” as an imposed label, a reclaimed project, and a racially narrowed colloquialism. “North Africa” is a modern geopolitical term masking a mixed reality of Arab-Islamic identity, Amazigh indigeneity, and the unifying exception of the Nile. It is within this tangled semantic field that the larger conflict plays out.
However fiercely identity may pull toward the Mediterranean and the Mashriq, North Africa remains wedded to the continent by chains of geography, history, and modern necessity. This is the objective, inarguable substrate upon which all subjective identities in the region have to contend with and integrate (sometimes unsuccessfully). North Africa rests upon the African tectonic plate; its mountains, faults, and mineral wealth are born of the same continental processes as the rest of Africa. The Sahara, so often imagined as a “barrier” is in fact a shared system which stretches from the Atlas foothills to the Sahelian shore. It is a connector that demands specialized adaptation, fostering trans-Saharan networks. Most definitively, the Nile River ( which we may call Egypt’s raison d’être) is an African river. Its lifeblood flows from the Ethiopian Highlands and the Great Lakes region. Egypt’s destiny is therefore irrevocably tied to the management, politics, and climate of the African interior. The historical tether cannot be ignored either, long predating the era of the Arab-Islamic. Ancient Egypt’s civilization cannot be understood outside its perpetual dialogue (whether through trade, warfare, and migration) with Nubia (aka modern Sudan), and cultures further South. The Kingdom of Kush conquered and ruled Egypt, a profound African reversal of power. Deeper in the desert, civilizations like the Garamantes (in modern Libya) built sophisticated subterranean irrigation systems and acted as crucial intermediaries, proving the Sahara was a space of commerce. Foundational to all is the Amazigh, the indigenous inhabitants whose presence for thousands of years constitutes the incontestable truth that the first “North Africans” were, and are, an African people.
The final link to “Africa proper” is the political-economic necessity of the 21st century. The various crises of the Sahel, where ever they come from (looking at you France), do not respect colonial borders, and spill northward. Meaning the instability in Mali or Niger becomes a direct security threat to Algeria, Tunisia, and Libya. Membership in the African Union also acts as a strategic platform for conflict mediation and collective bargaining. To abandon it would be to forfeit influence and abdicate responsibility for the systems which directly impact North African security and prosperity. North Africa is, in effect, bound by the objective fact sovereignty, which ties it to Africa. Thus, while a North African might look eastward toward Mecca in prayer, they stand atop African history and the African future of shared crises and fortunes. Any complete disavowal of “Africa” is a flight from the world. The land one stands on insists upon the very connection to Africa which some North Africans intend to disavow.
It’s strange, then, that the anchor is so very clearly African, but identity decisively points east. The allegiance of the region is certainly not to a continental project, it is towards the civilizational sphere of the MENA world as an active daily substance. It becomes less strange when one familiarizes themselves with a few “pillars” if you will. The first is the religion. Islam is the primary communal bond, and the foundational layer of social and spiritual life. Its culture and moral framework orient the believer towards the holy cities of Mecca and Medina in the Arabian Peninsula, and not towards the African interior. The Ummah is the imagined polity whose heartlands lie in Asia. The language goes hand in hand with Islam. Arabic is the language of the sacred text of the Quran, media, and politics. The lingua franca that ties a Moroccan student to a Lebanese novel, or an Algerian politician to a Qatari news (Al Jazeera comes to mind) is Islam. This shared linguistic worlds creates a continuity with the Mashriq, making Cairo and Beirut feel culturally closer than Bamako or Abuja. The daily cultural diet does not dissuade the continuity, either. The dominance of Arab satellite television (like the aforementioned Al Jazeera, MBC, etc.), and the circulation of Arab music, or Ramadan television dramas, creates a social world through the digital which faces East. One final factor to take into account is the historical context of Ottoman rule or influence, which brought centuries of integration into an Eastern empire, and the later anti-colonial struggle framed through the potent ideology of Pan-Arabism. Gamal Abdel Nasser or Ba’athist parties are dreams of Arab unity, not of African unity. The political imagination stands as horizontally Arab, not African.
Not African and not Arab? Not realistic. This consolidation of MENA identity conditions an active dis-identification. “African”, often reductively coded as Black and sub-Saharan becomes the distinguishing other by which the social process that separates “us” from “them” takes place. This is heard in colloquial or casual speech and seen in social attitudes toward darker-skinned Africans. This attitude is reflected in media representations that subtly or overtly frame “Arab” North Africa as more civilized, developed, or historically significant than “Black” Africa. The distancing taking place here is hierarchical, and acts as a mechanism which maintains the coherence of the preferred self-conception as being solely in continuity with the Middle East, and coincidentally being placed in Africa. Thus, when asked about identity, the heart’s answer is drawn from this shared reality of politics, religion, culture, language, media, and so on and so forth, all flowing from the East. The African ground, for all its material weight, remains for many, a silent geographical contingency, overshadowed by the life of Arab-Islamic civilization. This is the powerful subjective truth that any honest analysis must confront.
With respect to this, we have a contradiction at the crux of North Africa’s identity. The vehement distancing from “Africa” is not evidence by a lack of relationship, it is the loudest pronouncement of its unresolved relationship with it. One does not build an identity against something that is irrelevant. The force of the rejection mirrors the depth of the historical “entanglement”, which is putting it lightly. The primary source of this ordeal is found in the Trans-Saharan Slave Trade. For over a thousand years, this civilizational-scale process crafted a brutal social world where the “African”, particularly the Black African from south of the desert, became synonymous with “other” and “servile” in North African societies. This process embedded a structural and psychological anti-Blackness into the fabric of the social relations, establishing a hierarchy of human value that placed the Arabized or Amazigh North African above the “abeed” (slave, a term still used derogatorily). This is the origin of the separation, a hierarchy native to the nation, but certainly reinforced by European colonialism. French and Italian administrators, armed with the pseudoscience of racial anthropology, pursued explicit “Mediterraneanist” or “Berberist” policies. They argued that North Africans (particularly Amazigh populations) were a white and noble race, distinct from and superior to the Negroes of sub-Saharan Africa. This attitude, being internalized by segments of the local elite, added a modern European-sanctioned racial taxonomy to the older Arabo-Islamic one. Being offered a bargain which North Africans could not refuse, they were to be placed on a higher rung of the colonial racial ladder, contingent on accepting their own differentiation from the content below. It is not a coincidence that the term here is bargain, harkening back to the Faustian Bargain, and the Heideggerian ‘forgetting of being” mentioned that North Africa has inherited from Europe. This is just one avenue to be explored.
In the modernist project of building independent nation-states, anti-Blackness became a functional “moment of differentiation”. For North African elites forging post-colonial identities, they required a “constitutive outside”, or an “other”, against which the modern national self could be defined. The constructed “Black African” serves this purpose perfectly. Defining the modern “Arab-Maghrebi” citizen involved a conscious (and in some cases unconscious) differentiation from this other, purifying the national-self image and aligning it with a perceived lineage of historic (Phoenician, Roman, Arab) Mediterranean civilization, in contrast to a stereotyped “primitive” and “tribal” Africa. We have now a contemporary performance of that centuries-old differentiation, a socio-political ritual that reinforces the borders of a non-African national identity. The scorn for the Black migrant is, tragically and ironically, a perverse reaffirmation of a separate self. North Africa’s “non-African” identity is, to a significant degree, built upon the history of its relationship with and by virtue of Africa.
To view this struggle as between Arab and African, is to miss the signals originating from North Africa itself which problematize this binary. We can look to the Amazigh Revival as an indigenous corrective. The fight for Tamazight language and culture is a reclamation of an African identity which predates Arabization by a thousand years. In this assertion, what is to be found is an assertion of North African identity that is uniquely African, yet distinct from Arab political hegemony and sub-Saharan cultures. Further south is the Sahara-Sahel zone, where one can interpret this land as a land of synthesis and differentiation- one which purist ideologies will be forced to categorize incorrectly, or count as a deviation from the norm of a worldview. Nations like Mali, Niger, and Sudan are crucibles where Arab and Amazigh North Africa has blended with Songhai, Fulani, and Nubian cultures for millennium. Timbuktu’s manuscripts, are written in Arabic script but preserve the knowledge of the Malian empire. This is a good example of “fusion”. These societies render the harsh distinction between northern littoral and southern forests moot.
Gaddafi Gaddafi’s Libya stands apart. Isolated in the Arab world, he declared himself “an African, catalyzing a decade of objective institutional creativity aimed at nothing less than the political and economic unification of the African continent. His government became the primary architect and financier of the African Union’s transformative leap from the loose OAU to a more integrated body. Libya funded over 15% of the AU’s budget, championed the creation of the African Investment Bank and the African Monetary Fund, and was the driving force behind RASCOM, the first satellite for African telecommunications, breaking a foreign monopoly. He leveraged state wealth to build infrastructure across the continent and positioned Libya as the patron for liberation movements and marginalized regions. Gaddafi represented a tangible, institutional answer to the question of continental unity that far exceeded the symbolic gestures of other North African states. Unfortunately, this objective reality being created existed in a stark parallel to a subjective social reality it failed to fundamentally alter, or rather, did not get to alter. While the state built African unity, Libyan society, which carried the historical baggage of the trans-Saharan slave trade and hierarchical social structures, continued to exhibit widespread anti-Black prejudice This was visible in the treatment of sub-Saharan migrants. Gaddafi’s Pan-Africanism did not constitute a national reckoning with racism or stem from a grassroots embrace of African identity. This paradox (which is in reality, the paradox of North African identity) reached its climax after the 2011 NATO-led intervention. The instant evaporation of the Pan-African project revealed its precarious foundation- that it was an edifice built on state power and not on broad objective spiritual conviction. The subsequent descent of Libya into chaos including the resurgence of open slave markets targeting Black Africans, and the collapse of living standards (from one of Africa’s highest literacy rates and GDP per capita to a failed state) demonstrated that Gaddafi’s noble project of unification was doomed to fail if it did not reconstitute North African identity, or reconcile what it actually means to be a North African. The very fact that slavery re-emerged tells you something intrinsically about the social substance of the Libyan people, and by extension North Africans as a whole. That the post-Gaddafi landscape did not revert to a neutral “Libyan” identity, instead regressing into the raw, unmediated societal pathologies and tribal factions that the state had previously contained and channeled, illustrates that this was a reversion to an older, unhealed spirit. In other words, the reappearance of slavery is the return of the repressed historical spirit, proof that the social substance of the people had not been transformed. Most, if not all countries (barre Congo and Sudan), have confronted what it means to be an African (particularly this or that kind of African- Angolan, South African, Ethiopian, Kenyan, etc.), and so this brings a sense of closure to sub-Saharan countries that is felt in contrast when dealing with the North African countries. This is part of why Africans south of the Sahara may find it easier to forget North Africa, because they have not dealt with this intrinsic anti-Blackness. After all, besides Gaddafi’s attempt and the practical establishment of the OAN (of which Morocco left in 1984 as a protest for the OAN recognizing the SADR. It must also be stated that Western Sahara was not part of historic Morocco in any meaningful sovereign sense, and so leaving the OAN in 1984, then applying for membership in the EEC is not a good look), the only interface with North Africans that sub-Saharans have is from North Africans arguing why they should be able to say the n-word. There is no real nationally recognized effort in reconstituting any kind of North African identity.
Gaddafi’s project, therefore, was not wrong in its vision, but premature in its historical moment. It demonstrated that objective institutions cannot survive if they outpace the zeitgeist. One can say this was a failure of synchronization. The projects’ destruction demonstrates for us that the central unresolved problem for North Africa is existential. What does it mean to be North African? Until this question is answered in a way that reconciles body and spirit, heaven and earth, all grand political architectures in North Africa will remain vulnerable. The future of North Africa as an integral part of Africa does not hinge on more trade agreements or AU resolutions, though these are necessary. It hinges on an existential, spiritual reckoning that has yet to occur, one that is to be found in a collective and conscious confrontation with the historical that created its dis-identity. Until it does, North Africa will remain what it is today: the continent’s estranged shore, physically present but spiritually separated, building its identity upon the forgetting of its deepest and most intimate connection. The path belonging to North Africa is not eastward, it is inward, into a history it must remember down into the plains and forests across the desert, and inward into itself into a self it has yet to become.
title: Libya under Gaddafi: The Only African Country in North Africa?

content:
Intro After writing my recent article Epistle to Polygenism, a few insights came to mind regarding the human form and it’s arrangement, going hand in hand with an elaboration of the relationship between man and animals. This article is on this consideration. In this undertaking, the process describing the generation of embodied form these beings take is necessary, namely, an explication of “evolution”, which is the name for this process. In its conventional usage, “evolution” refers generally to the gradual physical transformation of species over geological time, driven by variations and natural selection (which is a catch-all term employed to describe a failure for an organism to reach biotic potential). This framework has provided the world with a few insights, surely, yet it leaves more basic questions unanswered. Why did self-consciousness emerge only in one lineage? What is the inner relationship between the human form and the animal forms that so closely resemble it? Why does the fossil record show long periods of status punctuated by sudden appearances, rather than the smooth continuum the theory might predict?
To address these, we must expand our understanding of evolution to include dimensions that the physical sciences, by their very methodology, cannot access. Spiritual-scientific research offers precisely such an expansion. In this account, evolution is not merely the history of bodily forms. It involves the evolution of the planet, of spirits, or the ideas and concepts by which perceptions are generated by, the whole of nature, and so on and so forth. Thus far, the only theory in materialistic sciences approaching such a conception is the Expanding Earth hypothesis, which can explain everything modern plate tectonics does except for not taking as an assumption the fixed radius of the Earth over time. In addition, it also answers questions such as the relatively young age of the ocean floor, and a few others. Besides this theory, modern materialist science treats nature as dead, and if not dead, then it is dead at the moment of observation, put into hypostasis for the thinking subject. Evolution as properly understood involves the spiritual hierarchies who guide cosmic and earthly development, dimensions of evolution such as Conditions of Life, Form, and Consciousness, planetary stages, and involution and sacrifice wherein higher spiritual beings descend into lower states to create conditions for humanity’s ascent. Evolution can be said to be sustained by a cosmic sacrifice. Within this framework, the relationship between humanity and the natural kingdoms, for instance, does not appear to us as one of linear emergence. It is a stratified separation. Telos is also involved. If something is developing, there is something it develops towards, and so the spiritual science’s understanding of evolution does not end with the present, nor does it end with biological organisms. Everything in nature is alive, as Schelling states. However, this will be elaborated on at a further date. The question concerning us today is along the following: Why do we look like apes?
Monkey Humans and great apes have a strikingly similar skeletal structure. The arrangement of limbs, the opposable thumbs, the facial musculature, and so on, have long been held up as the crowning evidence for Darwinian evolution. When Huxley, Darin’s “bulldog”, famously toured the British Isles with a chimpanzee skeleton, his message was very clear. The human body is merely a modified ape body according to him. The resemblance is so close that comparative anatomy leaves little room for doubt. We share a common ancestor, the ape form is our past, and the human form is a more refined variation of the same basic blueprint, problem solved. This argument has become the bedrock of modern evolutionary biology.
Note: The entire field of evolution tends to be pseudo-science, lacking all forms of explanatory value or normativity. Whether evolutionary psychology or biology, it does not matter. What a trait in one organism is said to be an adaptation, the respective opposite trait in another is also said to be a beneficial adaptation. For instance, a dull color in a bird may be beneficial in order for it to survive and reach biotic potential (reproduce). Within the same species bright-colored feathers is explained away as beneficial for attracting mates and reaching biotic potential. If any trait can be rationalized post hoc, its explanatory power becomes null. More on this: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Just-so_story. While the counterargument to this statement is that an emphasis on different selective pressures resulting in fitness is the main point might seem attractive, further probing reveals that fitness (survival and reproduction) becomes a tautological loop of reproduction. “What reproduces is more fit, and the fittest survive” becomes “those reproduce more reproduce more”. Evolutionary psychology is similarly a fruitless domain parading itself as normative theory.
The resemblance of monkey to man is elegant, parsimonious, and seemingly supported by a wealth of genetic and fossil data. Yet for all its explanatory power, it still rests on the assumption that morphological similarity necessarily implies genealogical descent. In other words, because we look like apes, we must have evolved from them. This is an unjustified assumption that will find no solution in the materialistic sciences. Modern neo-Darwinian synthesis is ultimately a theory of form. It explains how populations adapt to environments through variation and natural selection, supposedly. There are disagreements, but we can grant this for the sake of argument. We may also grant that it successfully accounts for the diversification of species, the distribution of traits, and the patterns of genetic relatedness. The human-ape similarity fits into this framework, that we share a primate body because we inherited it from a primate ancestor, with modifications that accumulated along separated linages. However, the limit is reached precisely at the threshold of what makes us human. The aforementioned theory cannot explain why, among all primates, only one lineage developed self-consciousness, the capacity for abstract thought, moral reflection, language, art, and so on. It treats these as emergent epiphenomena of increased brain size, yet it has no account of why the brain expanded in this lineage alone, or how mere quantity of neurons could ever produce the qualitative leap to the reflexive “I”. The evolutionary biologist is left with what Thomas Nagel coined “the hard problem” of consciousness. This is a problem that neo-Darwinian synthesis and natural selection was never equipped to solve.
Additionally, Darwinism (as shorthand) treats the animal kingdom as a series of steps leading *coincidentally to man. The ape is positioned as our nearest relative, a kind of unfinished version of ourselves. This narrative implicitly carries a hierarchy, but it cannot ground that hierarchy in anything beyond differential reproductive success. Morality, art, philosophy, and language all become byproducts of a brain that happened to get larger, with an unclear object in mind, and in some cases denies the object is there at all. The human form is reduced to a contingent outcome of mutation and selection, with no intrinsic meaning or direction.
Spiritual science offers a fundamentally different reading of the same anatomical facts. In this view, the human being is the original form. Not in the sense of a physical ancestor, but as an archetypal spiritual reality that preceded the physical separation of the kingdoms of nature. Long ago, during periods of times named the “Lemurian” and “Atlantean” epochs, the human physical body was still malleable and had not yet solidified into the fixed skeletal form we know today. At various stages of this long process of solidification, certain beings that guide species from the astral plane (called group souls) took hold of physical bodies that had prematurely hardened. These bodies, which had once been part of the human stream, became the foundations for the animal kingdom. The apes represent the last such separation, occurring when the human form was already quite close to its final shape. This is why their anatomy so closely resembles ours. They are a kind of photograph of the human form at the moment it branched away. In this conception, a portion of developing humanity “lags behind” and becomes the basis for a new natural kingdom. The animals, plants, and minerals are not our ancestors; they are our own former possibilities frozen in time.
The ape, therefore, is not a primitive ancestor, but a side branch. It is a being that took on the physical form that the human stream had at a particular stage, but then stopped developing further. The anatomical similarity cannot be evidence that we evolved from apes, it is instead evidence that apes and humans share a common origin in the plastic proto-human form which existed before the final hardening of the human body.
If this reading is correct, then the fossil hominins (australopithecines, Homo habilis, Homo erectus, and even earlier forms) take on a different significance, representing various side branches or transitional forms that either preceded the full descent of the human “I” into physical bodies, or were left behind as the human form continued to refine and harden. As mentioned in my Polygenism article, the fact that stone tools appear with australopithecines shows that even before the “I” fully incarnated, beings with a degree of intelligence and will were interacting with the physical world. These beings, in Steiner’s account, were ensouled by group souls (or by individual souls not yet fully independent) who were preparing the physical “body” for the eventual incarnation of the self-conscious, reflexive “I”. The appearance of anatomically modern Homo sapiens in the Quaternary period (precisely when the Out of Africa population emerges) marks the completion of the hardening process. The physical body is stably mineralized and capable of hosting the “I”.
Conventional evolutionary theory, exactly because it limits itself to physical form and a vague notion of genetic inheritance, cannot accommodate the above picture. It has no language for telos, ideality, freedom, self-determination, etc. Yet these are precisely the dimensions needed to answer the questions that Darwinism leaves open. Why does consciousness exist? Why only in humans? Ultimately, what is the relationship between the human form and the animal form that surrounds us? Spiritual science certainly does not reject the empirical findings of paleontology, genetics, taxonomy studies, or comparative anatomy. Instead, it offers a broader view for these findings, one that interprets the same data in a fuller way and recontextualizes the information accordingly in a unified way. The ape-human similarity then transforms from an animal origin to a human origin.
Influence and Development In order to understand the human-animal relationship, let us set aside the image of evolution as a linear chain from simple to complex, of ape to man. In its place, we must envision a process. Rationalize this process however you want; the Hegelian abstract to concrete, the alchemical process, separation to hardening to inward transformation, etc. are ways to understand the procedural moments here. This is a process that spans beyond the history of the Earth as we currently understand it into the period of time known as the middle Lemurian epoch (roughly 359–299 million years ago). If the “archetype” of man was abstract (spiritual, so to say) at some point, then this is the period it began to “harden” and manifest a body in the world for itself (in addition to the beginning of the sex distinction, among others). The human form was not yet the fixed structure we know today. Steiner describes it as “plastic” often, “soft”, and still directly shaped by the will. Bones had not yet fully mineralized, the body was more fluid, more responsive to the inner life of the soul, as a necessary condition for the incarnation of the human “I” in the world. During this epoch, the human being moved through the world with a body that was, in essence, a sculpture in process. The forces that would later become the distinct animal, plant, and mineral kingdoms were still contained within the human form. The human being was, as Steiner puts it, a kind of “potential totality” from which all other terrestrial forms would eventually be separated. It is worth noting that the Earth and man concurrently “evolve”, however, we are focused on man and animals for now.
The Atlantean epoch continued this process. The body remained more fluid than today. For example the skeletal system was still not fully solid. Yet a progressive hardening was underway. The first really durable artifacts appear only at the very end of Atlantis, marking the threshold where human will began to encounter a world that was itself becoming mineralized. The great disaster and flood that ended Atlantis finalized this transition. The human form, now sufficiently hardened, could enter the current post-Atlantean epoch as a stable vessel for the fully incarnated “I”. Why did the human form need to harden exactly? Because the “I” requires a respective stable instrument. A body that is too plastic cannot reliably support the kind of self-conscious reflection that defines true humanity. The hardening of the skeleton, the mineralization of the bones, the stabilization of the brain structure, etc. are the invented culmination of a long spiritual preparation. The fossil record, when read this way, reflects this gradual solidification. The reflexive, self-conscious human “I” did not always dwell in a physical body though. During earlier planetary stages of Earth’s development, the human soul moved among the spiritual hierarchies, gradually preparing for embodiment. The descent into a physical form in one sense is considered a sacrificial act. The higher “I” bound itself to a body that would limit it, in order for it to gain the capacity for freedom, self-knowledge, love, etc. This descent did not happen all at once. In the Lemurian epoch, the “I” began to inhabit the still plastic human form, but was not yet fully integrated. During the Atlantean epoch, the “I” deepened its connection, but still the body was not entirely stable. Only in our epoch did the “I” achieve full-self consciousness within a fully hardened body.
This brings us to today. If the animal kingdom consists of forms that separated from the human stream at various stages, then the human being accurately is more than the synthesis of animal traits as a being who inwardly transformed what animals externalized. This is the principle of metamorphosis which Goethe recognized in plants where leaf transforms into sepal, petal, stamen, etc., and in animals, where the vertebrae transformed into skull bones. This extends into the whole of evolution. The human form is a metamorphosed unity of what appears in the animal kingdom as separate, specialized organs.
Consider the fact that what in animals is an external organ becomes an internal organ in humans. The fish’s swimming bladder, which regulates buoyancy, is metamorphosed into the human eardrum. The fish’s gills become the tiny bones of the ear. What was once spread out in water becomes concentrated and internalized. What in animals is a specialized function becomes a unified capacity in humans. Steiner talks in depth on the siphonophore, a colony of distinct individuals each performing a single function such as balance, feeding, movement, reproduction, or defense. This is a kind of deconstructed human. In the human being, all these functions are drawn together into a single self-conscious organism, held together by the “I”. When the “I” fails to hold them together, we see the fragmentations of mental illness, a kind of return, in distorted form, to the condition of the siphonophore. One may also consider that a one-sided development becomes a balanced threefoldness in humans. Animals are, as Steiner says, “human beings which have remained stationary in the one-sided development of their characteristics”. The ant has developed social organization to an extreme, but lacks individuality. The beaver has developed engineering wisdom, but cannot innovate. The human being, in contrast, holds thinking, feeling, and willing, in a balance that requires the insecurity and openness that the one-sided animal lacks.
This inward transformation certainly didn’t happen automatically. It was guided by spiritual beings working from the astral plane. Since animals don’t have an “I”, they are guided by animal group-souls, beings on the astral plane who direct the collective intelligence of the species (think the beaver’s dam, the bird’s nest or migration, and the bee’s hive). The third elementary Kingdom works directly on animal forms, shaping them according to instincts and desires. In the human being, these formative forces are internalized. The same forces that shape the animal from without become, in the human, the inner forces of conscience and self-direction. In other words, the human “I” learns to do consciously what the group soul does instinctively.
The ape occupies a special place in this picture. It separated from the human stream last, when the human form was already close to its final shape. Its anatomy is therefore most similar to ours for this reason, because it froze at the stage just before the human form achieved its full uprightness, the capacity to receive the “I”, and so on. Moreover, Steiner notes that the ape “brings too much from the group soul down into its own individual existence”. Unlike lower animals, which fully return to the group soul at death, the ape retains something in its physical organization that cannot go back. This gives it a quality that appears almost individual- a something that resembles the human “I”, but is in fact a stalled potentiality. This residue becomes, in the spiritual world, the elementals called salamanders, beings that are neither fully human, nor fully animal, lingering as a kind of unfinished creation.
Everything is for a purpose or, the form of man The animal kingdom consisting of potentials separated from man is on the one hand. The other hand focuses on what the animal kingdom is a derivation of: the human form. Then human form can thus be understood as the living synthesis of these potentials, more than the sum of the parts. It contains these potentials by having turned them inside out, under the sovereignty of the “I”. Among all features that distinguish the human body, three stand out as especially revealing. First is the upright posture, then the articulate larynx, and finally, the free hand with its opposable thumb. Each of these is a metamorphosed animal organ, an inward transformation of what in the animal kingdom appears as an external structure.
The most visible difference between humans and the higher animals is the vertical orientation of the spine. In the animal, the spine runs horizontally. The head is attached at an angle, its weight being carried by the forelimbs. In the human being, the spine stands upright, and the head balances freely atop it. For conventional biology, this is a simple and mere addendum to bipedalism, one that freed the hands for tool use. For spiritual science, that which subsumes the material sciences, it is the physical expression of the “I”. Steiner explains that the upright posture serves as a necessary condition for self-consciousness, in addition to it being a mechanical advantage. When the spine is horizontal, the astral body flows predominantly in the direction of the Earth’s surface, anchoring consciousness in instinct. But when the spine becomes vertical, the astral body can orient itself toward the cosmos, allowing the “I” to descend into the physical body. The upright human being stands as a column connecting heaven and earth, as a microcosmic counterpart to the cosmic order.
The uprightness is also the basis for the threefold division of the human organism into the nervous system, rhythmic system, and metabolic limb-system mentioned in a previous article on the development of human physiology and spirituality. Each of these three systems have their own relation to the animal kingdom, meaning in the animal, these functions are often spatially separated into distinct organs, or even distinct creatures. In the human being, they are drawn together into a single upright form, integrated by the “I”. The upright posture makes possible a second transformation via the metamorphosis of the air-organism into the organ of speech.
Consider a bird. Its entire organization is oriented toward the air, with its hollow bones, its lungs connected to air sacs extending throughout the body. The bird’s song is produced by a syrinx that vibrates with the breath. It is, in a way, an instrument of the air elementals, being shaped by the element it moves through. The song can be considered an instinctive yet direct expression of the group soul. During the Lemurian epoch, when the human form was still plastic, one possible evolutionary path was to become a bird-like being. Had the human stream talent that direction, we would have become creatures of the air, our whole organization lightened and specialized for flight, and our consciousness, merged with the astral currents of the atmosphere. But that path was not taken. Instead, the human being concentrated the air element into the organ of the larynx.
What in the bird is spread throughout the whole human body as the air sacs, pneumatic skeleton, and functional song, is in the human being inwardly concentrated. The larynx is, as Steiner describes, an inverted or “transformed” bird, it is an inverted air organ. It is the place where the air is shaped, and where breath becomes articulated sound, and subsequently where that sound becomes another instrument of the “I”. This inversion explains why the upright posture is so intimately connected with speech. Only when the spine is vertical and the head is balanced can the larynx develop the flexibility necessary for articulate language. The ape, which walks only partially upright, has a larynx that remains a call organ. Capable of signalling emotion, surely, but incapable of the nuanced articulation that goes hand in hand with thought.
The last metamorphosis we will concern ourselves with is the hand. In animals, the forelimb is specialized for a single function. The hoof is used for running, the wing for flight, the paw for grasping, the fin for swimming, and so on. Each is a perfect adaptation to a specific environment, but each is also one-sided. In the human being, the forelimb is notably unspecialized, it is the universal organ that can become a toolmaker, a painter, a gesture of blessing a caress, and similarly a strike, an insult, a warrior, etc. The universality here is a result of a double metamorphosis. Steiner explains that the human hand is not simply an unmodified animal paw or this or that, recall that the human archetype takes precedence. The specialized forelimbs are in actuality the one-sided determinations of the human hand. It is in reality the inward integration of what in the animal kingdom is distributed across different forms. The strength of the hoof, dexterity of the paw, lightness of the wing- these are all potentials concentrated into the hand. The human hand is free because it has not been forced into any single function. It can learn any function because it is guided by the “I”. The opposable thumb is the physical sign of this freedom. In the animal, the digits are often locked into a fixed pattern. In the human, they are arranged to meet one another, to create a circuit of touch and manipulation that unironically mirrors the circuit of self-consciousness.
Evolution is a constant process of turning structures inside out, and concentrating and diluting functions. “Human” is thus the category of being which holds as its characteristic positionality the metamorphosed unity of all animal potentials. As Goethe intuited, the human being is the “sum” of the animal kingdom, although this should not be read literally. It is through a transformation of each animal quality into a higher human faculty, and a gradual falling away and one-sidedness of these same qualities in man that we should view the animal kingdom in relation to us. We should come to re-cognize the lion’s courage as moral strength, the eagle’s vision as piercing insight, and the serpent’s cunning as wisdom.
Possible forms, roads untaken, and form clarified Mentioned previously was how we could have taken an evolutionary path resulting in a bird-like form. As an avid lover of sci-fi, a question arises for me. What other forms could have emerged? The body we inhabit today is the successful result of a process of differentiation and avoidance. Guided by higher spiritual being, the human stream was consciously sculpted from many potential animal-like outcomes. Each of these potentials, had it had been allowed to harden, would have procured a creature as distinct from us as the ape is, yet each would have carried within it a parietal reflection of what the human being was meant to become. These alternative forms remain in their separated kingdoms, as reminds of the choices embedded in the process of evolution. Steiner explicitly mentions several such potentialities.
In the early Lemurian epoch, before the head had fully differentiated as the organ of self-consciousness, the human form was structured very differently, that is to say that the metabolic-limb system was dominant. The head, by contrast, was relatively undeveloped, more like an appendage than any sort of sovereign centrality. If this configuration had hardened, the result would have been a being of immense physical strength but dull consciousness; a giant. This potential is associated with the cyclopean beings of old, creatures of immense power who lacked the refinement of individual thought, with their singular eye representing a symbol of a unified and undifferentiated consciousness. This potential was avoided through the development of the larynx and the eventual specialization of the nervous system, centered in the head. The human being, instead of becoming a brute giant, concentrated the forces of will and movement into the limbs, while developing the head as the organ of self-reflection. The giant today remains a memory of what could have been.
At another stage, a different influence predominated. Beings associated with the air element (sylphs and their spiritual guides) worked strongly on the human form, offering a potential path toward the description previous of lightness and flight. Had the human stream taken this direction, our whole organization would have become quite pneumatic. We would have hollow bones, air sacs throughout the body, sing rather than speak, and have an instinctive and dispersed consciousness, fully merged with the group soul. We would have become beings of air. This potential was separated off and became the bird kingdom. The human being did not become a bird, as you can tell. Instead, the air element was concentrated into the aforementioned larynx, resulting in a spiritualized version of the air potential.
Another major potential is the one that concerns us most directly, since it produced the mammals, and among them, the ape! At the stage when the human form was already approaching its final shape, a hardening occurred. Certain beings (influenced by the Luciferic and Ahrimanic forces) could not wait for the full transformation. Their physical organization solidified prematurely, producing a form that mimics the human structure in a fixed, specialized way. Why did the ape not complete the journey? Precisely because of the temptation of Luciferic and Ahrimanic beings. During the Lemurian epoch, Luciferic beings aimed to bestow self-awareness prematurely, while Ahrimanic beings aimed to harden and calcify form before the “I” was ready to descend. The poor ape was grabbed these forces and solidified at the threshold. Its body reflects a state of a soul that is *trapped. Imitative and cunning, but tied to the physical-etheric world, carrying within it the outlines of an individuality never attained. This is why the ape has always held a strange fascination. When one gazes upon the face of an ape, they witness trapped intelligence.
The human form was guided away from these potentials by the hierarchy of spiritual beings working to consciously sculpt the human form. The Lords of Form (Exusiai) held the human archetype in the spiritual worlds, preventing it from solidifying prematurely. The Spirits of Motion (Dynamis) directed the formative forces away from one-sided developments. Even the human “I”, before its full descent, exerted a longing for a form that could house self-consciousness. The result is a body that is uncompromising in its need for freedom. The animal kingdom, likewise, is the family of forms that could have been us, had we not been guided toward something higher.
Implications and the future The relationship posed before us has implications that extends beyond the past and into the future. Mention was made of the evolution of the Earth, matter, nature (including physics), and spiritual beings, however, we will still focus on man and animal. But it is worth noting that these will change in the future as well.
The principle of animal forms arising as separated potentials does not end with the Lemurian Atlantean, or current post-Atlantean epoch. It is a recurring dynamic, one that will be simultaneous with the evolution of humanity through successive planetary stages. The Earth is destined to undergo further transformations, with the next being called “Jupiter”, “Venus”, and then “Vulcan”. At each stage, the category of being “human”, one who is the central being of Earth evolution, will develop new faculties, and new kingdoms will branch off from the human stream. For example on the future Jupiter condition, Steiner indicates that what is currently the animal kingdom will have evolved into a kind of intermediate kingdom, reflection the separated potential of a humanity with a highly realized “I”. Meanwhile, new animal-like beings will separate from humanity. These will be beings who, during our current stage of Earth evolution, did not fully integrate the “I” into their physical and etheric bodies. The group souls that now ensoul animals will have advanced, while some human souls (or perhaps aspects of human souls) will devolve into group-soul existence, forming new natural kingdoms. Ponder this: the fact that supersensible human faculties will be developed, and that the principle of animal engendering is still occurring, suggests to us an animal kingdom in the future that will reflect an animal kingdom comprising the potentiality of supersensible perception. Psychic-apes residing in an intermediate stage of “man” and “animal” is not a discounted possibility in the future.
We may also take into consideration that the extinction of a species is not only an ecological event. It may play a part, but it is largely a reflection, and sometimes a consequence of, the general spiritual posture of humanity. When a species disappears, its group soul withdraws from physical manifestation. In some cases, this withdrawal is part of the natural rhythm of evolution. Maybe the group soul has completed its task and moves on to new forms. In other cases, extinction is accelerated, or even caused by human activity. This carries spiritual significance.
Take the passenger pigeon, which was once the most numerous bird in North America, driven to extinction in the early 20th century by industrial scale hunting and habitat destruction. Or the great auk, the thylacine, or the great American buffalo (which is not extinct just yet!), or any of the countless species lost in the wake of human expansion. From the perspective of spiritual science, these extinctions reflect a spiritual posture that treats the natural world as a standing reserve, one that severs the human being from the kingdoms that were once contained within him.
When a species goes extinct, its group soul does not perish. It does however withdrawal from the physical plane, and continues in the spiritual world, sometimes waiting for a future epoch when conditions are right for re-embodiment. But the manner of its withdrawal is shaped by humanity’s relationship to it. A species treated with indifference or hatred carries those qualities into its spiritual afterlife. A species that is honored and loved leaves behind a different seedling. This places a huge responsibility on humanity. Extinction defined as the loss of biodiversity is a bankrupt definition. The true meaning is that it is a spiritual event which reflects our own inner state. When allowing a species to vanish, we are, in a sense, amputating part of ourselves, that part of ourselves which is a potential that we have refused to recognize or redeem. The current wave of extinctions driven by human activity can be considered a mirror, showing us a humanity that has forgotten kinship with the natural world and hardened itself against the very kingdoms that once made its own evolution possible.
Now, one might object that if animals are only “separated potentials”, why do we see a clear progression from simpler to more complex forms in the fossil record? Why do later animals appear to possess more developed consciousness (sentience) than earlier ones? The direction of the process is important here. The materialist standpoint sees complexity and apparent consciousness as proof of evolution’s power, as if the process itself were gradually producing mind. From the spiritual scientific perspective, which encompasses the materialistic sciences and includes philosophy (hence the grounding of any possible deductive knowledge), the direction is reversed. Matter is the sediment of consciousness, and the increasing complexity of animal forms over geological time is the vestigial remnants of a gradual revelation of potentials that were always present in the spiritual world.
When we see a modern animal with complex social behavior or problem-solving skills, we’re witnessing the descending arc of group-soul consciousness, which was always there, now becoming more visible as the physical forms that expresses it become higher in definition. The animal does not approach humanity, rather, the group soul, over vast periods, expresses itself through forms that are increasingly capable of reflecting its wisdom. Materialist evolution, lacking this framework, will naturally interpret the same facts as evidence of a continuous and blind upward-driving process. It will bring up dolphins, the empathy of elephants, crows which solve problems, monkey which use grunts and body language to communicate, and so on as proof that consciousness is an emergent property of complex nervous systems. But this interpretation mistakes the how for the why. Still, there are those who would, in the future, see the new kingdoms engendered by man as proof of evolution. If they still believe in materialist nonsense by then, they will be relegated to the 8th sphere, becoming the animals they so desperately want to see as approaching humanity.
The human form in the future will also evolve and metamorphose further. I will bring up the larynx just one more time. This organ, which currently serves speech and song, is destined for a further transformation. Steiner describes that on the future Jupiter condition (a stage of Earth’s evolution), the human larynx will evolve into an organ of generation/creation in a much higher sense than it currently is. The word will become directly creative, as it was in olden times, only now unified. What is now spoken will then be, carrying with it the power of actualization. This future capacity will not be automatically given to all, however. Those human beings who do not properly develop the necessary faculties, such as misusing speech and hardening their hearts, will have this potential “hardened” or “split off”. They will become the seed for a new kingdom, just as the apes and other animals arose from those who could not wait for the full descent of the “I”. Yet again, future animal kingdoms will be crystallized human potentials.
Evolution is presented to us as a spiral. What happened in the past on the physical level will happen in the future on a more spiritual level. The animals of the future will not be identical to today’s animals, but they will be externalized group soul expressions of human capacities that were not individually developed. Likewise, we have before us an ethical responsibility. By developing our higher faculties, especially the “I” in freedom and love, we are also preventing the unnecessary fragmentation of the human form into new separated kingdoms. Conversely, what we neglect or pervert (or refuse to transform) can become, in future ages, a new animal-like race. The choices we make today are shaping the forms that will surround our posterity beyond.
title: Man, Animal, and Evolution: The Relationship Finally Clarified

content:
Intro The featured header image above depicts the figures of Tristan and Isolde. Their story is one that, on the surface, appears to be a medieval tale of romance and forbidden love. Tristan, a brave Cornish knight, is sent to Ireland to escort Isolde, an Irish princess, to marry his uncle, King Mark. During the journey, they accidentally drink a strong potion meant for Isolde and mark on their wedding night. The potion turns out to be a love potion, a potion which induces, or suggests, strong feelings of love toward another. As a result, it binds them in an irresistible passion. Despite marrying King Mark, Isolde and Tristan begin a secret affair, fraught with danger and suspenseful situations. Eventually they are discovered and forced to flee. The story ends in tragedy, with one version finding a wounded Tristan sending for Isolde, the only one who can hear him. He arranges for the ship carrying her to fly a white sail if she is aboard. His jealous wife lies, telling him the sail is back. Believing Isolde isn’t coming, Tristan dies of despair, Isolde arrives, finds him dead, and dies of grief beside him. In one sense, the story does tell of a forbidden love, in the sense that the true meaning behind the veil of this story is esoteric and not immediately accessible. To enter into this narrative is to observe an allure, drawing one into the familiar territory of the living forces within the human soul. Within Tristan one finds the consummate achiever, a master of sword and strategy. His world comprises action, conquest, and honor. However, beneath the armor resides a loneliness which no comrade’s praise can fill. When encountering Isolde, it is less a meeting of two people, and much more of a “completion”. He finds within her the embodiment of fulfillment. Likewise, his desire for her is a desperate, spiritual thirst mistaken for a worldly one. He is the masculine spirit, proficient in extending dominion over the shape of the external world, being encountered by a wave of unfamiliarity in feeling and soul. It would be correct to say that his tragedy is the belief that wholeness lies in another, turning love away from communion into fatalistic ends. Contrast this with Isolde, who begins as a figure defined by her function. She is the “other” to which Tristan remedies himself with. Archetypically, she can be considered a healer. And so, when ingesting the potion, both figures find themselves awakening to a different law in their hearts. In Tristan, Isolde finds the incarnation of the romantic, the opposite of the worldly ordered world of obligation (particularly since princess marriages were for political aims and not borne of true love). Her journey descends into the sovereignty of the heart, where all bonds and identities dissolve into a unitary connection. This is the mirror of the feminine dilemma; women find themselves resisting the urge to completely surrender themselves to Eros, where love becomes the totality of existence, in effect erasing the self it initially wanted to fulfill. Contained within these two figures is an immense magnetism, where each polarity contains one half of totality, and at once what the other needs. Yin-yang, being-non-being, day-night, sun-moon, and so on and so forth. Man exists as citizens of both regions, oscillating between each. To contemplate upon this fact of reality is to ask oneself, “what, essentially, are man and woman?”. Is their polarity merely some biological necessity, or social determination? Or does it point towards something more fundamental in the structure of human being? It is here we may begin an exploration with a distinct and, some might argue, provocative lens. Behind the veil or romance, this story points towards the dynamics foundational to man’s existence. Because the human spirit evolves through direct being and participation, existence itself is structured by the polarities of activity and receptivity, or any of the various polarities which define respective modes of being such as thought and feeling, or outward formation and inward space. To become “whole”, consciousness must fully understand (and inhabit, however this mechanism of reincarnation and spiritual economy is beyond the scope of this article) it’s opposite. The tension and attraction between what we call masculine and feminine principles is the primary schoolhouse for this education, and as such, Tristan and Isolde represent unconscious enactments of this law. Much time can be spent on any number of interpretations, dissecting this or that aspect of the story, etc., but our principal aim in this article is “male and female”. Correspondingly, we can define “male and female” or “man and woman” as the complementary modes through which a genderless human consciousness experiences earthly life in this period of time. Let this not be confused with static identities, for they are temporary orientations which themselves have been flipped and turned inside out at times.
Contemporary problems hold back our understandings of “man and woman”. Modern discourse on gender, for all its analytical rigor, operates within a framework of lacking. When it comes to sex, it can describe differences, whether through the lens of biological essentialism, or social constructivism, but it cannot ultimately answer why these differences should matter in any existential sense. At best, a conception can be developed which relies on the natural immediacy of the sexed distinction, out of which social institutions make use of this content and fashion what they will from it- typically the family structure which interfaces with civil society and the market. However, this lacks a freely determined telos for sex, let alone gender, which we can consider briefly as the configuration of polarities in the makeup of man (and of which we will deal with later). What all modern views lack is a coherent and unifying telos.
First, modern conceptions lack a meaningful account of sexual difference itself. They can catalogue distinctions as either dictated by genes or culture, but what of the archetypal magnetism that arises from these distinctions? The lack of explanation for that animating force allows many “New Age” beliefs, or any belief which is subjective and lacks rigor, to fill in the blanks. Without a purpose, a reason why these dynamics exist, difference is fated to be reduced to either a mechanical fact (of which mechanistic and chemist modes of being are to be used by something higher. This includes the solipsistic mechanism of the Concept, Hegelians aren’t excluded!) or arbitrary construction, rendering the draw between masculine and feminine principles as a byproduct of biological reproduction, or social fiction. A second lack is a sustainable model for the integration of the body and consciousness. The materialist view, for example, makes the body a cage of determinism, worshiping its god “chance”. Constructivism likewise treats the problem of consciousness as incidental. Neither satisfactorily explains how our desires, drives, or identities interact with our embodied reality. We are left with a gap between what we are told we are as an organism, or merely as a social subject, and what we experience ourselves to be as conscious beings inhabiting a gendered form with personal (and spiritual) urgency. A third lack can be the lack of a framework which reconciles universal patterns and infinite personal variation. What destiny, to be caught between the false choice of rigid categories, and relativity so total that it obliterates the very possibility of a common ground which it is generated by? These failures result in an intelligibility. We are unable to speak meaningfully about a common human experience shaped by these two polarities, while also honoring the novelty, or unique journey, of every individual.
Ultimately, and most importantly (for the philosophically minded), modern conceptions lack a telos. The most significant absence in our current understandings is what I initially mentioned- telos. By denying any inherent purpose or end goal to the masculine-feminine polarity, human relationships and gender identity are severed from any higher, ideal, cosmic, spiritual development. This can help describe the crisis of meaning we face today, or rather, this attitude is a byproduct of modernity. I surely don’t mean to imply that “solving gender roles” (read: resorting to dogmatic and blind traditionalism) will help us resolve the “crisis of meaning” in modern day. What I wish to underscore is that if this fundamental aspect of human being has no purpose beyond utility or subjective expression, then agonies and pleasures, such as those expressed in the tale of Tristan and Isolde, no matter how beautiful or sublime, are coincidences. Our longing for completion is dead on arrival, being rendered biochemical or social accidentals.
So, we will solve this problem. Not comprehensively, I can only provide suggestions and clarifications with the help of anthroposophy. For it to be a comprehensive solution, there is the implication that the reader takes it upon themselves to live the upcoming wisdom, in one form or another. I will post a “too long; didn’t read” section for those that wish to find a log line to orient themselves around:
TL;DR The claim that there is “no difference” between men and women is misleading, it relies on a higher meaning that simply isn’t there. It is true that the “I”, or ego, of a person is genderless, but to claim this in the face of immediate biological differentiation is invoking an esoteric knowledge that the masses imply don’t intend to convey. On the other hand, insisting that these differences are representative of a difference in kind, rather than mode, is even stupider. Men and women are much more alike than they are different. We are capable of the same intellectual and creative feats, barre certain physiological predispositions. Where they often diverse is in their mode of engagement with their world, or their way of “interfacing”. I personally know women who grasp Hegel (the logico-intellectual composer of this age par excellence) more intuitively and intellectually than many of my peers in groups dedicated to understanding Hegel, however, only one actively sustains the research that German Idealism demands. This is less about capacity and more about orientation. Our current “intellectual” climate is not biased toward men per se, but towards a more “masculine” mode of interfacing; through abstraction, systematizing, compartmentalization, etc. When that mode is treated as neutral, half of human experience is filtered out. I suppose it must also be said that marginalization should not be accepted, if a member of the female sex/gender wants to be a girlboss CEO, her merit should be proven through the fruits she bears - although there are some important qualifications to make in the “feminism” section of the article below.
The Story To move beyond our current impasse, we can turn to the framework of spiritual science found in anthroposophy. Such an approach does not contradict empirical observation, but does seek to understand into the realm which gives meaning and context to what we observe in the sensible world. It helps provide exactly what is missing via a coherent teleological narrative of human development in which sexual polarity plays a part.
Firstly, we will begin with “human”. To speak of “man” is to invoke more than a biological species; it is a designation of a universal category of being defined by the potential for self-conscious spirit and moral freedom in the context of a partnership with the cosmos. This potential is our shared telos, the inherent destination inscribed in our nature. The human being is thus a process of becoming, and describes the latent capacity to realize being-hood. This journey of course in not uniform. The very structure of a developmental process, where a universal potential seeks realization under conditions of freedom and through the resistance of matter, necessitates a spectrum of manifestation. Within the human family, this appears as a divergence in the pace and degree of awakening. Some individuals, through intensified activity and under certain cosmic and karmic conditions, crystallize the human telos earlier and more fully. They become the forerunners, those in whom the future form of humanity becomes partially visible today. They act as translators for higher possibilities into forms that can gradually be comprehended and integrated by the broader collective. On the other hand, the same freedom that allows for accelerated development implies the possibility of arrest. Others may remain more firmly embedded in earlier, more instinctual stages of the human archetype. They are the echoes of humanity’s past forms, persevering strengths and memories that the advancing vanguard party might otherwise lose in its forward striving. What is being described here is an intrinsic feature of a cosmos in evolution, and the feature of any class of being which undergoes true evolution (involving telos). There are those that are advanced, those that are on the right track, and those that lag behind. Between these three points exists a gradient, in the same way that a single seed produces a field of plants at different stages of bloom. The one human telos manifests across a field of consciousness in varying stages of realization.
Since we have established our telos as the universal potential for spirit to become self-aware and free, it is appropriate to discuss the core of this being as the “I”, or the ego. The ego is the genderless seat of consciousness. Its telos is to awaken to its own spiritual nature and participate in the evolution of the world. This potential cannot be realized abstractly and must be realized in the world, and this requires a journey of individuation, and a descent into a kind of “limitation” which forces it to develop self-awareness and moral capacity. This is where the differentiation into masculine and feminine principles becomes essential. The creation of two distinct, but complementary, modes of embodied existence is the next topic.
The cosmology of anthroposophy is derived from Rudolf Steiner’s clairvoyant (which has a stricter definition than colloquially understood) investigation of the “Akashic Record”. If in the middle of reading this “story” stemming from anthroposophy, it starts to become unbelievable, you can skip to a section where I extrapolate the logical and structural takeaways as it relates to gender and sex, or, alternatively, treat this all as metaphor. This research into the “Akashic Record” describes, among many things, human evolution across vast epochs and stretches of time. In this view, the current physical form of humanity is the result of a long process of densification and specialization. He posits that in a remote past named the Lemurian epoch (which I argue should be called Thermian), presented a world alien to what we know now, characterized by a state of fluidity and malleability that permeated the Earth and its life forms. The Earth was described as a semi-fluid, thick substance, compared to a “fairly thick muddy sauce or soup”, or a “thickish liquid”. The atmosphere was much denser than it is today, water was also “much more fluid”. Physical malleability was key, seeing as the human body and other organisms consisted of “soft plastic matter” because the Earth’s crust was not yet hardened. Forms could be shaped and changed more easily by will and spiritual forces. Plant and animal life existed in archaic, often gigantic forms. What are now small ferns were then mighty trees, forming vast forests. The animal kingdom was dominated by creatures of an “amphibious” character. The most prominent descriptions provided are of giant, slime-like organisms. One source describes “giant slime oysters” or jelly-like creatures so vast that “all of France could have easily fit” on their backs (talk about escargot). Since we are focused on man for the time being, we shall turn our attention there and inward. I only wished to portray a kind of equilibrium between being and the environment that being resides in, or in logical form a kind of commensurability with global and local, revealing their unity.
The human being existed in an androgynous/bisexual state. The physical body was soft and plastic, malleable towards the self-determined forces of man and the environment (this, according to Steiner, helped in the formation of organs, which is important for later). Many world religions and mythologies preserve the memory of humanity’s original bisexual state. For example, the oldest Ancient Greek gods were described as having two sexes; in the mystery temples, only hermaphroditic figures were present. The Zoroastrian Myth portrays the memory even clearer through its narratives. So what of reproduction? Well, reproduction was a process of self-fructification, as opposed to the dualistic act we are familiar with. Self-fructification is an influential concept to understand, because prior to the sexed distinction, modes of reproduction up until the middle of the “Lemurian” period evolved through several distinct non-sexual stages, culminating in the birth of biological sexuality as we know it. The modes progressed from the immediacy of self-generation, or sprouting, where the “astral” human” incorporated etheric matter into its form, grew, and then split, similar to a bud sprouting. It then continued as humans were still unified with what would later become bird and lower mammal species. The early Lemurian period still saw a “spiritualistic” reproduction, where reproduction occurred through the forces of Kama (astral desire/feeling), which was still dissolving in the general environment, and not yet individualized. In spiritual science, the figures of Adam Kadmon, from Hebrew esotericism, and the Giant Ymir, from North mythology, are preserved clairvoyant memories of a literal, prior state of being. These figures represent a lost primal unity between humanity, the Earth, and all life. Both myths depict a time when the Earth itself was a giant human form (and remember, this is not “man” as it is currently, human is a designation). Accordingly, the killing of Ymir, from whose body the world is fashioned (his flesh becoming Earth, bones becoming mountains, etc.) symbolizes the cosmic process of densification and differentiation. It is the death of this original unity. As the Earth’s matter condensed and cooled, the one unified being had to separate to allow for further evolution. This is a literal fact, according to spiritual science. To the casual reader, I can only appeal to its structural coherence, that the fact that in its earliest stages the entire etheric substance of the Earth consisted of undifferentiated human archetypes makes perfect logical sense.
Let us return again to the form of man in Lemuria, particularly on the nature of the soul of man. The human soul was inherently male-female (androgynous). In this early state, the soul could completely express its complete, bisexual nature directly onto the soft physical matter of the body. This is how sexual expression was related to man in those times. Regarding sexual reproduction, because each being contained both principles, reproduction was an internal process of self-fructification, as previously mentioned. A human being could bring forth another from within itself, without the need of a partner. The form of consciousness in man had humanity living in a dream-like, or picture-consciousness. People perceived the world primarily through hearing and touch, experiencing it as a flow of images and natural forces rather than clear, conceptual thoughts. They lacked memory, and a capacity for what we now call thinking.
The primary catalyst for the origin of the sexed distinction, this process of division, was the increasing density and solidification of the Earth’s substance. As matter became harder and more rigid, the soul could no longer perfectly impose its own bisexual (male-female) image upon it. Likewise, the physical form was forced by external nature to specialize. It could no longer sustain the full expression of both soul principles (Will and Imagination) in one form. Thus, it split into two specialized vessels. The male aspect of the soul was associated with Will (Iccha Shakti), and the female aspect with Imagination (Kriya Shakti or the rudimentary beginning of thought, with thought being described here as an inner generative process). With this new form, a loss of self-fructification (the force that was once directed entirely outward for reproduction) occurred, and was partially blocked. This surplus energy was redirected inward. When one considers that self-fructification can be read as reproduction, and follow that reproduction does not only mean sexual reproduction, but the upkeep and maintenance of being, the implication for this formative energy as that which builds and sustains the physical organism and drives reproduction becomes clearer. Previously, this force was entirely engaged in the act of self-generation and sustaining the plastic form of older man. As matter condensed and the body specialized into male or female form, this “one way street” became a dead end, so to say. An individual could no longer complete the creative circuit alone. A portion of this formative energy now existed as surplus to the external requirement of solitary reproduction, and was dammed up within the organism. Seeing as it was “dammed up”, this energy looked for a new outlet and so it turned inward and upward, becoming the building substance for a new interiority (the refined organization necessary for thought, memory, and individuated consciousness as ego). Steiner describes this as the fructification of the human being by the Spirit.
The middle of the Lemurian epoch brought about the division of sexes from hermaphroditic, or androgynous man, for this reason. Another connection one may bring up is that the transition from the dreamy, picture-like consciousness (where one lived within spiritual images and directly perceived natural forces) to a sensory, brain-bound consciousness is the structure of the “Fall” of man, of his necessary descent into matter. The soul, to gain self-awareness had to build a physical body, with specialized sense organs, redirect creative energy inward to form a brain, and begin to rely on the senses and, eventually, logical thought to construct the “picture” of the world. There are a few notable consequences to this “Fall”. The first is the loss of intuitive knowledge. Humanity, as a whole, lost the old, instinctive clairvoyance and direct perception of spiritual beings and natural forces. The rudimentary emergence of the ego as a result of the withholding of the formative energy/soul-force inward (due, again, to the densification of the world and of man) resulted in the creation of an interior world for man. This separation from the outer world led to self-interest, or selfishness (and by extension desire). Likewise, man’s striving for inward development became selfish. This selfishness will play an interesting part in our story soon enough. On the other hand, there is a positive side. With isolated selfhood and sensory-based inner activity came the capacity for free choice, personal judgment, and moral responsibility. This is otherwise known as the true origin of Good and Evil. Man was no longer purely instinctive or guided by direct spiritual impulse.
To recap for the sake of brevity, a division of man from bi-sexuality to uni-sexuality occurred. The two sexes developed along different, but complementary paths, particularly in early Lemurian society. The “Masculine Path” (Will) focused on mastery over external natural forces and the physical world. Education for boys involved extreme hardening and endurance to strengthen the will and kill the carnal, freeing the will-power (Iccha Shakti). Men were seen as conduits for divine, natural powers. The “Feminine Path” (Imagination/Memory) focused on absorbing inner meaning and the spiritual qualities of nature. Education for girls cultivated a dreamy imagination, and a calm receptivity in the face of natural beauty and terror, such that they should serve as poised conveyors of what nature says. Within temples, initiates engaged in direct “intercourse with the Beings who work at the building of the world itself”. This gazed into the “creative laboratory of Nature”, perceiving and participating in the spiritual forces behind the world. This experience consecrated the initiates (who, again, were female), and their very presence exerted a natural branded magical influence on others. It is not explicitly stated that this activity was segregated by sex, but it does establish that women developed the essential faculties that would qualify them for such work. Through this, women developed the first germs of memory, and the earliest moral concepts of “good and evil”. They became the first interpreters of nature and the originators of a more personal, inner life.
The Âkâshic Records reveal what are in this respect scenes of much beauty. One of these shall be described. We are in a wood close to a gigantic tree. The sun has just risen in the east. Mighty are the shadows thrown by the palm-like tree across the cleared space round it. With her face to the east, and in a state of exaltation, we discern a priestess on a seat prepared of curious natural objects and plants. Slowly and in a rhythmic cadence flow from her lips certain wondrous sounds which are repeated again and again. Ranged in large circles, a number of men and women sit round her with dreamy faces, absorbing inner life from the sounds. Still other scenes may be witnessed. At another place, arranged in like manner, a priestess chants in a similar way, but her tones have in them something more mighty, more powerful, and the men around her move in rhythmic dances.
